components.english: I think many people do like giving an opinion about politics, especially when political decisions affect their daily lives directly.
components.chinese: 我認為，很多人的確喜歡對政治發表意見，尤其當政治決定會直接影響他們的日常生活時更是如此。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think many people do like giving an opinion about politics, especially
when political decisions affect their daily lives directly.
[我認為，很多人的確喜歡對政治發表意見，尤其當政治決定會直接影響他們的日常生活時
更是如此。]

components.english: The reason is quite simple: politics is not just something that happens in parliament or on the news. It shapes things like housing, education, healthcare, taxes, and even people’s sense of fairness. So, when an issue hits close to home, people usually feel they have skin in the game. In that sense, giving an opinion is not only about debate; it is also about feeling heard.
components.chinese: 原因其實很簡單：政治並不只是發生在議會裡，或者新聞中的事情。它會影響住屋、教育、醫療、稅收，甚至人們對公平的感受。所以，當一個議題和自己的生活息息相關時，人們通常都會覺得自己是局中人。從這個角度來說，發表意見不只是為了辯論，也是在爭取被聽見。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The reason is quite simple: politics is not just something that
happens in parliament or on the news. It shapes things like housing, education,
healthcare, taxes, and even people’s sense of fairness. So, when an issue hits close
to home, people usually feel they have skin in the game. In that sense, giving an
opinion is not only about debate; it is also about feeling heard.
[原因其實很簡單：政治並不只是發生在議會裡，或者新聞中的事情。它會影響住屋、教育、
醫療、稅收，甚至人們對公平的感受。所以，當一個議題和自己的生活息息相關時，人們通
常都會覺得自己是局中人。從這個角度來說，發表意見不只是為了辯論，也是在爭取被聽

components.english: For example, if the government changes transport fares, public services, or education policy, ordinary people are very likely to talk about it with friends, family members, or colleagues. Even people who do not usually follow politics closely may suddenly have a lot to say when the issue touches their wallet or their future.
components.chinese: 例如，如果政府調整交通費、公營服務，或者教育政策，一般人很可能都會和朋友、家人，或者同事談論這些事情。即使平時不算特別關心政治的人，一旦事情牽涉到自己的荷包或未來，也往往會突然間有很多意見。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if the government changes transport fares, public
services, or education policy, ordinary people are very likely to talk about it with
friends, family members, or colleagues. Even people who do not usually follow
politics closely may suddenly have a lot to say when the issue touches their wallet
or their future.
[例如，如果政府調整交通費、公營服務，或者教育政策，一般人很可能都會和朋友、家人，
或者同事談論這些事情。即使平時不算特別關心政治的人，一旦事情牽涉到自己的荷包或
未來，也往往會突然間有很多意見。]

components.english: So yes, I would say a lot of people do like expressing political opinions, particularly when the topic strikes a nerve or feels personally relevant.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我會說很多人確實喜歡表達政治意見，特別是當話題觸動他們，或者和自身利益密切相關的時候。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, I would say a lot of people do like expressing political
opinions, particularly when the topic strikes a nerve or feels personally relevant.
[所以，是的，我會說很多人確實喜歡表達政治意見，特別是當話題觸動他們，或者和自身
利益密切相關的時候。]

components.english: That said, not everyone enjoys talking about politics, because it can be divisive, exhausting, and sometimes a bit of a minefield.
components.chinese: 不過，並不是每個人都喜歡談政治，因為政治有時很容易令人對立、感到疲累，而且某程度上也像一個地雷區。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, not everyone enjoys talking about politics, because it can be
divisive, exhausting, and sometimes a bit of a minefield.
[不過，並不是每個人都喜歡談政治，因為政治有時很容易令人對立、感到疲累，而且某程
度上也像一個地雷區。]

components.english: Some people would rather steer clear of political discussions because they do not want arguments, awkwardness, or damaged relationships. Politics can open a can of worms very quickly, especially when people hold strong views and are not willing to listen. On top of that, some people feel that political talk goes round in circles and changes very little, so they simply cannot be bothered.
components.chinese: 有些人寧願避開政治討論，因為他們不想引發爭吵、尷尬，或者傷害彼此關係。政治話題很容易一下子把一堆麻煩都打開，尤其當大家立場很強，而且不願意聆聽對方時更是如此。除此之外，也有人覺得政治討論常常只是在原地兜圈，實際上改變不了多少，所以他們乾脆不想多談。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: Some people would rather steer clear of political discussions
because they do not want arguments, awkwardness, or damaged relationships.
Politics can open a can of worms very quickly, especially when people hold
strong views and are not willing to listen. On top of that, some people feel that
political talk goes round in circles and changes very little, so they simply cannot
be bothered.
[有些人寧願避開政治討論，因為他們不想引發爭吵、尷尬，或者傷害彼此關係。政治話題
很容易一下子把一堆麻煩都打開，尤其當大家立場很強，而且不願意聆聽對方時更是如此。
除此之外，也有人覺得政治討論常常只是在原地兜圈，實際上改變不了多少，所以他們乾脆
不想多談。]

components.english: For instance, at a family dinner or a gathering with friends, someone may deliberately avoid bringing up politics because they know it could sour the mood within minutes. In that case, silence is not a lack of opinion; it is more a way of keeping the peace.
components.chinese: 例如，在家庭聚餐或朋友聚會裡，有人可能會刻意避免提起政治，因為他知道這個話題可能幾分鐘之內就會把氣氛弄僵。在這種情況下，沉默並不代表沒有看法，而更像是一種維持和氣的方式。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, at a family dinner or a gathering with friends,
someone may deliberately avoid bringing up politics because they know it could
sour the mood within minutes. In that case, silence is not a lack of opinion; it is
more a way of keeping the peace.
[例如，在家庭聚餐或朋友聚會裡，有人可能會刻意避免提起政治，因為他知道這個話題可
能幾分鐘之內就會把氣氛弄僵。在這種情況下，沉默並不代表沒有看法，而更像是一種維持
和氣的方式。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say many people do like giving an opinion about politics, but whether they speak up often depends on the setting, the people around them, and how safe they feel doing so.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說很多人其實都喜歡對政治發表意見；不過，他們會不會真的說出口，往往取決於當下的場合、身邊是甚麼人，以及他們是否覺得在那種情況下表達意見是安全的。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say many people do like giving an opinion about
politics, but whether they speak up often depends on the setting, the people
around them, and how safe they feel doing so.
[總結來說，我會說很多人其實都喜歡對政治發表意見；不過，他們會不會真的說出口，往
往取決於當下的場合、身邊是甚麼人，以及他們是否覺得在那種情況下表達意見是安全

question.english: Is changing opinions good for society?
question.chinese: 改變意見對社會有好處嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Is changing opinions good for society?
改變意見對社會有好處嗎？

components.english: Yes, in general I think changing opinions can be very good for society.
components.chinese: 是的，整體來說，我認為改變意見對社會可以是很有好處的。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, in general I think changing opinions can be very good for society.
[是的，整體來說，我認為改變意見對社會可以是很有好處的。]

components.english: The main reason is that a healthy society should be able to learn, reflect, and correct itself. If people never changed their minds, society would stay trapped in old habits and outdated beliefs. New evidence, new experiences, and new voices often help people see things differently, and that can lead to better decisions and fairer attitudes. In that sense, changing your opinion is not necessarily a sign of weakness; quite often, it is a sign of growth.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，一個健康的社會應該有能力去學習、反思，以及修正自己。如果人們從不改變想法，社會就很容易被困在舊習慣和過時觀念之中。新的證據、新的經歷，以及新的聲音，很多時都能幫助人們從不同角度看事情，而那往往會帶來更好的決定和更公平的態度。從這個角度來看，改變意見未必是軟弱的表現；很多時候，它反而是成長的表現。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that a healthy society should be able to learn,
reflect, and correct itself. If people never changed their minds, society would
stay trapped in old habits and outdated beliefs. New evidence, new experiences,
and new voices often help people see things differently, and that can lead to
better decisions and fairer attitudes. In that sense, changing your opinion is not
necessarily a sign of weakness; quite often, it is a sign of growth.
[主要原因是，一個健康的社會應該有能力去學習、反思，以及修正自己。如果人們從不改
變想法，社會就很容易被困在舊習慣和過時觀念之中。新的證據、新的經歷，以及新的聲音
，很多時都能幫助人們從不同角度看事情，而那往往會帶來更好的決定和更公平的態度。從
這個角度來看，改變意見未必是軟弱的表現；很多時候，它反而是成長的表現。]

components.english: For example, when people become more informed about issues like mental health, disability, or social inequality, they often begin to move away from harsh or simplistic judgments. That kind of shift can make society more understanding and more humane.
components.chinese: 例如，當人們對精神健康、殘疾，或者社會不平等等議題有更深入了解之後，他們很多時就會慢慢放下那些苛刻而片面的判斷。這種轉變能令社會變得更有理解，也更有人情味。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, when people become more informed about issues like
mental health, disability, or social inequality, they often begin to move away
from harsh or simplistic judgments. That kind of shift can make society more
understanding and more humane.
[例如，當人們對精神健康、殘疾，或者社會不平等等議題有更深入了解之後，他們很多時
就會慢慢放下那些苛刻而片面的判斷。這種轉變能令社會變得更有理解，也更有人情味。]

components.english: So yes, I would say changing opinions is generally good for society, because it allows society to move forward instead of standing still.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我會說改變意見整體上對社會是有益的，因為它能讓社會向前走，而不是停在原地。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, I would say changing opinions is generally good for
society, because it allows society to move forward instead of standing still.
[所以，是的，我會說改變意見整體上對社會是有益的，因為它能讓社會向前走，而不是停
在原地。]

components.english: That said, I do not think every change of opinion is automatically positive.
components.chinese: 不過，我並不認為每一次改變意見都一定是正面的。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think every change of opinion is automatically
positive.
[不過，我並不認為每一次改變意見都一定是正面的。]

components.english: It really depends on why people are changing their minds. If they do so because they have listened carefully, thought deeply, and responded to better evidence, that is a very healthy thing. But if they change opinions too quickly because of rumours, social pressure, or whatever happens to be fashionable that week, that can make society unstable and shallow. A society needs open minds, yes, but it also needs a backbone. Otherwise, public opinion can blow about like a weather vane in every passing storm.
components.chinese: 這其實很取決於人們為甚麼會改變想法。如果他們是因為願意細心聆聽、深入思考，並且回應更好的證據而改變意見，那就是一件很健康的事。但如果他們改變得太快，只是因為流言、群體壓力，或者那個星期剛好流行甚麼，那就可能令社會變得很不穩，也很膚淺。一個社會當然需要開放的頭腦，但同時也需要一條脊骨。否則，民意就很容易像風向標一樣，在每一場風暴裡東倒西歪。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: It really depends on why people are changing their minds. If
they do so because they have listened carefully, thought deeply, and responded to
better evidence, that is a very healthy thing. But if they change opinions too
quickly because of rumours, social pressure, or whatever happens to be
fashionable that week, that can make society unstable and shallow. A society
needs open minds, yes, but it also needs a backbone. Otherwise, public opinion
can blow about like a weather vane in every passing storm.
[這其實很取決於人們為甚麼會改變想法。如果他們是因為願意細心聆聽、深入思考，並且
回應更好的證據而改變意見，那就是一件很健康的事。但如果他們改變得太快，只是因為流
言、群體壓力，或者那個星期剛好流行甚麼，那就可能令社會變得很不穩，也很膚淺。一個
社會當然需要開放的頭腦，但同時也需要一條脊骨。否則，民意就很容易像風向標一樣，在
每一場風暴裡東倒西歪。]

components.english: For instance, if someone changes their view after hearing another side of the story, that can build wiser public discussion. However, if large numbers of people swing from one extreme to another just because of online outrage or emotional headlines, that usually creates more noise than insight.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人是在聽完事情的另一面之後才改變看法，那麼這種改變其實能帶來更成熟的公共討論。不過，如果大量人只是因為網上的情緒化輿論或煽動性標題，就由一個極端跳到另一個極端，那往往帶來的只是更多噪音，而不是更多見識。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if someone changes their view after hearing another
side of the story, that can build wiser public discussion. However, if large
numbers of people swing from one extreme to another just because of online
outrage or emotional headlines, that usually creates more noise than insight.
[例如，如果一個人是在聽完事情的另一面之後才改變看法，那麼這種改變其實能帶來更成
熟的公共討論。不過，如果大量人只是因為網上的情緒化輿論或煽動性標題，就由一個極端
跳到另一個極端，那往往帶來的只是更多噪音，而不是更多見識。]

components.english: Overall, I think changing opinions is good for society when it comes from reflection, humility, and evidence. It helps society grow wiser. But changing opinions for no solid reason is another matter entirely. The best society is not one that never changes its mind, nor one that changes it every five minutes, but one that knows how to rethink without losing its balance.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我認為當改變意見是來自反思、謙遜，以及證據時，它對社會就是有好處的，因為它能令社會變得更有智慧。但如果改變意見沒有紮實理由，那就是另一回事了。最好的社會，不是一個從不改變想法的社會，也不是一個每五分鐘就改一次的社會，而是一個懂得重新思考，同時又不失去平衡的社會。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I think changing opinions is good for society when it
comes from reflection, humility, and evidence. It helps society grow wiser. But
changing opinions for no solid reason is another matter entirely. The best
society is not one that never changes its mind, nor one that changes it every five
minutes, but one that knows how to rethink without losing its balance.
[總結來說，我認為當改變意見是來自反思、謙遜，以及證據時，它對社會就是有好處的，因
為它能令社會變得更有智慧。但如果改變意見沒有紮實理由，那就是另一回事了。最好的社
會，不是一個從不改變想法的社會，也不是一個每五分鐘就改一次的社會，而是一個懂得重
新思考，同時又不失去平衡的社會。]

question.english: On what occasions do people usually need to wait?
question.chinese: 在甚麼情況下，人們通常需要等候？

source_lines.text: Topic: On what occasions do people usually need to wait?
在甚麼情況下，人們通常需要等候？

components.english: I’d say people usually need to wait when a service is limited, shared by many people, or handled on a first-come, first-served basis.
components.chinese: 我會說，當某種服務資源有限、很多人共同使用，或者採取先到先得的安排時，人們通常都需要等候。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I’d say people usually need to wait when a service is limited, shared by
many people, or handled on a first-come, first-served basis.
[我會說，當某種服務資源有限、很多人共同使用，或者採取先到先得的安排時，人們通常
都需要等候。]

components.english: In everyday life, that happens all the time. People often have to wait for public transport, medical appointments, restaurant tables, government services, or customer service. The reason is fairly simple: when too many people need the same thing at the same time, a queue is almost inevitable. In that sense, waiting is just part and parcel of modern life.
components.chinese: 在日常生活裡，這種情況其實一直都在發生。人們常常要等公共交通、醫療預約、餐廳座位、政府服務，或者客服回應。原因其實很簡單：當太多人在同一時間需要同一樣東西時，排隊幾乎是無可避免的。從這個角度來看，等候其實已經是現代生活的一部分。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: In everyday life, that happens all the time. People often have to
wait for public transport, medical appointments, restaurant tables, government
services, or customer service. The reason is fairly simple: when too many people
need the same thing at the same time, a queue is almost inevitable. In that sense,
waiting is just part and parcel of modern life.
[在日常生活裡，這種情況其實一直都在發生。人們常常要等公共交通、醫療預約、餐廳座
位、政府服務，或者客服回應。原因其實很簡單：當太多人在同一時間需要同一樣東西時，
排隊幾乎是無可避免的。從這個角度來看，等候其實已經是現代生活的一部分。]

components.english: For example, in Hong Kong, people may have to wait for a train during rush hour, queue at a popular restaurant, or sit in a clinic waiting for their name to be called. None of that is unusual at all. In fact, for many people, it is just another ordinary slice of the day.
components.chinese: 例如，在香港，人們可能要在繁忙時間等地鐵、在受歡迎的餐廳排隊，或者坐在診所裡等叫名字。這些情況一點也不罕見。事實上，對很多人來說，這只是一天裡很平常的一小部分。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, in Hong Kong, people may have to wait for a train
during rush hour, queue at a popular restaurant, or sit in a clinic waiting for
their name to be called. None of that is unusual at all. In fact, for many people,
it is just another ordinary slice of the day.
[例如，在香港，人們可能要在繁忙時間等地鐵、在受歡迎的餐廳排隊，或者坐在診所裡等
叫名字。這些情況一點也不罕見。事實上，對很多人來說，這只是一天裡很平常的一小部

components.english: So, one common kind of waiting happens in daily situations where demand is high and time or space is limited.
components.chinese: 所以，其中一種很常見的等候，就是出現在那些需求很大、但時間或空間有限的日常情況裡。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one common kind of waiting happens in daily situations
where demand is high and time or space is limited.
[所以，其中一種很常見的等候，就是出現在那些需求很大、但時間或空間有限的日常情況

components.english: That said, people also need to wait in less visible ways, especially when they are waiting for an outcome rather than standing in a physical queue.
components.chinese: 不過，人們也會以另一種較不明顯的方式去等候，特別是當他們等的是一個結果，而不是實際站在隊伍裡的時候。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, people also need to wait in less visible ways, especially when
they are waiting for an outcome rather than standing in a physical queue.
[不過，人們也會以另一種較不明顯的方式去等候，特別是當他們等的是一個結果，而不是
實際站在隊伍裡的時候。]

components.english: I’m thinking of things like exam results, job interview outcomes, visa applications, deliveries, or important decisions from other people. That kind of waiting can actually feel harder, because you cannot always do much except sit tight and hope for the best. In other words, some waiting happens with your feet, but some happens with your mind.
components.chinese: 我想到的是像考試成績、求職面試結果、簽證申請、送貨安排，或者別人作出的重要決定。這種等候有時反而更難受，因為你很多時候除了耐心等下去，然後希望有好結果之外，其實做不了太多事。換句話說，有些等待是腳在等，但有些等待是心在等。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: I’m thinking of things like exam results, job interview
outcomes, visa applications, deliveries, or important decisions from other
people. That kind of waiting can actually feel harder, because you cannot always
do much except sit tight and hope for the best. In other words, some waiting
happens with your feet, but some happens with your mind.
[我想到的是像考試成績、求職面試結果、簽證申請、送貨安排，或者別人作出的重要決定。
這種等候有時反而更難受，因為你很多時候除了耐心等下去，然後希望有好結果之外，其實
做不了太多事。換句話說，有些等待是腳在等，但有些等待是心在等。]

components.english: For instance, a student may wait anxiously for exam results, or a job seeker may keep checking their phone after an interview. They are not standing in line anywhere, but they are still very much in a state of waiting. Sometimes that kind of silence can feel longer than any queue.
components.chinese: 例如，一位學生可能會很焦急地等考試成績，而求職者則可能在面試後不停看手機。他們雖然沒有實際站在哪條隊裡，但其實仍然處於等待之中。有時候，那種安靜無聲的等待，甚至比任何隊伍都讓人覺得漫長。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, a student may wait anxiously for exam results, or a
job seeker may keep checking their phone after an interview. They are not
standing in line anywhere, but they are still very much in a state of waiting.
Sometimes that kind of silence can feel longer than any queue.
[例如，一位學生可能會很焦急地等考試成績，而求職者則可能在面試後不停看手機。他們
雖然沒有實際站在哪條隊裡，但其實仍然處於等待之中。有時候，那種安靜無聲的等待，甚
至比任何隊伍都讓人覺得漫長。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say people usually need to wait both in practical situations and in emotional ones. Whether it is a train, a doctor, a result, or a reply, waiting tends to appear whenever something important is not yet in your hands.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，人們通常既要面對實際層面的等候，也要面對情感層面的等候。無論等的是列車、醫生、結果，還是回覆，只要某樣重要的東西還未真正到你手上，等待往往就會出現。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say people usually need to wait both in practical
situations and in emotional ones. Whether it is a train, a doctor, a result, or a
reply, waiting tends to appear whenever something important is not yet in your
hands.
[總結來說，我會說，人們通常既要面對實際層面的等候，也要面對情感層面的等候。無論
等的是列車、醫生、結果，還是回覆，只要某樣重要的東西還未真正到你手上，等待往往就
會出現。]

question.english: Who behaves better when waiting, children or adults?
question.chinese: 等待時，孩子和成年人誰表現得比較好？

source_lines.text: Topic: Who behaves better when waiting, children or adults?
[等待時，孩子和成年人誰表現得比較好？]

components.english: Generally speaking, I would say adults tend to behave better when waiting.
components.chinese: 一般來說，我會說成年人在等待時通常表現得比較好。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Generally speaking, I would say adults tend to behave better when
waiting.
[一般來說，我會說成年人在等待時通常表現得比較好。]

components.english: The main reason is that adults usually have more self-control and a better understanding of social rules. They may still feel impatient, of course, but they are generally more able to keep a lid on it. Children, by contrast, often find waiting dull, frustrating, or even unbearable, especially if they do not fully understand why they have to wait in the first place. In that sense, patience is a bit like a muscle, and adults have usually had more time to build it.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，成年人通常有較強的自我控制能力，也更明白社會規範。當然，他們也會感到不耐煩，但一般來說，他們更能把情緒收住。相比之下，孩子往往會覺得等待很無聊、很煩，甚至難以忍受，尤其是當他們根本不太明白自己為甚麼要等的時候。從這個角度來說，耐性有點像肌肉，而成年人通常有更多時間把它鍛鍊出來。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that adults usually have more self-control
and a better understanding of social rules. They may still feel impatient, of
course, but they are generally more able to keep a lid on it. Children, by
contrast, often find waiting dull, frustrating, or even unbearable, especially if
they do not fully understand why they have to wait in the first place. In that
sense, patience is a bit like a muscle, and adults have usually had more time to
build it.
[主要原因是，成年人通常有較強的自我控制能力，也更明白社會規範。當然，他們也會感
到不耐煩，但一般來說，他們更能把情緒收住。相比之下，孩子往往會覺得等待很無聊、很
煩，甚至難以忍受，尤其是當他們根本不太明白自己為甚麼要等的時候。從這個角度來說，
耐性有點像肌肉，而成年人通常有更多時間把它鍛鍊出來。]

components.english: For example, if people are waiting in a long queue, most adults will just check their phone, look around, or stand quietly, whereas children are more likely to fidget, complain, or keep asking, “How much longer?”
components.chinese: 例如，如果人們正在排一條很長的隊，大多數成年人通常只會看手機、四處看看，或者安靜地站著；但孩子則更有可能不停扭動、抱怨，或者反覆問：「還要多久？」

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if people are waiting in a long queue, most adults will
just check their phone, look around, or stand quietly, whereas children are more
likely to fidget, complain, or keep asking, “How much longer?”
[例如，如果人們正在排一條很長的隊，大多數成年人通常只會看手機、四處看看，或者安
靜地站著；但孩子則更有可能不停扭動、抱怨，或者反覆問：「還要多久？」]

components.english: So, overall, I’d say adults usually behave better when waiting, mainly because they are better at managing boredom and frustration.
components.chinese: 所以，整體來說，我會說成年人在等待時通常表現得更好，主要因為他們較懂得處理無聊感和挫折感。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, overall, I’d say adults usually behave better when waiting,
mainly because they are better at managing boredom and frustration.
[所以，整體來說，我會說成年人在等待時通常表現得更好，主要因為他們較懂得處理無聊
感和挫折感。]

components.english: That said, I do not think adults always behave better, because some of them can be surprisingly impatient.
components.chinese: 不過，我也不認為成年人一定總是表現得更好，因為有些成年人其實也會出奇地沒有耐性。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think adults always behave better, because some of
them can be surprisingly impatient.
[不過，我也不認為成年人一定總是表現得更好，因為有些成年人其實也會出奇地沒有耐

components.english: Waiting gets on everyone’s nerves from time to time. Some adults may not throw a tantrum the way children do, but they can still become rude, irritated, or visibly annoyed. On the other hand, children can behave very well if they are well prepared, kept occupied, or clearly told what to expect. So the difference is not simply about age; it is also about personality, mood, and the situation itself.
components.chinese: 等待有時確實會令人心煩，這對任何人都一樣。有些成年人雖然不會像孩子那樣發脾氣，但他們仍然可能變得無禮、煩躁，或者把不耐煩全寫在臉上。另一方面，如果孩子事前有心理準備、有事情做，或者清楚知道自己要等甚麼，他們其實也可以表現得很好。所以，差異並不只是年齡問題，也和性格、心情，以及當下情況本身有關。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: Waiting gets on everyone’s nerves from time to time. Some
adults may not throw a tantrum the way children do, but they can still become
rude, irritated, or visibly annoyed. On the other hand, children can behave very
well if they are well prepared, kept occupied, or clearly told what to expect. So
the difference is not simply about age; it is also about personality, mood, and the
situation itself.
[等待有時確實會令人心煩，這對任何人都一樣。有些成年人雖然不會像孩子那樣發脾氣，
但他們仍然可能變得無禮、煩躁，或者把不耐煩全寫在臉上。另一方面，如果孩子事前有心
理準備、有事情做，或者清楚知道自己要等甚麼，他們其實也可以表現得很好。所以，差異
並不只是年齡問題，也和性格、心情，以及當下情況本身有關。]

components.english: For instance, a child waiting at a clinic with a book or a toy may stay calm for quite a while, while an adult in the same room may start sighing, checking the time every two minutes, or complaining about the delay.
components.chinese: 例如，一個在診所等候、手上有書或玩具的孩子，可能可以安靜地撐上一段時間；但同一個房間裡的成年人卻可能開始不停嘆氣、每兩分鐘看一次時間，或者抱怨等得太久。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, a child waiting at a clinic with a book or a toy may
stay calm for quite a while, while an adult in the same room may start sighing,
checking the time every two minutes, or complaining about the delay.
[例如，一個在診所等候、手上有書或玩具的孩子，可能可以安靜地撐上一段時間；但同一
個房間裡的成年人卻可能開始不停嘆氣、每兩分鐘看一次時間，或者抱怨等得太久。]

components.english: Overall, I’d still say adults tend to behave better when waiting, but not by default and not all the time. Sometimes children wear their impatience on the outside, whereas adults simply dress theirs up a little better.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我仍然會說成年人在等待時通常表現得較好，但那並不是天生必然，也不是每次都如此。有時孩子只是把不耐煩直接表現在外，而成年人則只是把那份不耐煩包裝得好一點而已。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, I’d still say adults tend to behave better when waiting,
but not by default and not all the time. Sometimes children wear their
impatience on the outside, whereas adults simply dress theirs up a little better.
[總結來說，我仍然會說成年人在等待時通常表現得較好，但那並不是天生必然，也不是每
次都如此。有時孩子只是把不耐煩直接表現在外，而成年人則只是把那份不耐煩包裝得好
一點而已。]

question.english: Compared to the past, are people less patient now? Why?
question.chinese: 和過去相比，現在的人是不是比較沒有耐性？為甚麼？

source_lines.text: Topic: Compared to the past, are people less patient now? Why?
和過去相比，現在的人是不是比較沒有耐性？為甚麼？

components.english: Yes, I think in general people are less patient now than they used to be, mainly because modern life has become much faster and more convenience-driven.
components.chinese: 是的，我覺得整體來說，現在的人普遍比以前更沒有耐性了，主要是因為現代生活節奏變得更快，而且愈來愈講求便利。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, I think in general people are less patient now than they used to be,
mainly because modern life has become much faster and more
convenience-driven.
[是的，我覺得整體來說，現在的人普遍比以前更沒有耐性了，主要是因為現代生活節奏變
得更快，而且愈來愈講求便利。]

components.english: These days, people are used to getting things almost instantly. They can order food in minutes, stream a film straight away, send a message in seconds, and search for information at the tap of a screen. When life starts moving at that speed, waiting begins to feel unnatural. In a way, technology has trained people to expect life on fast-forward, so even small delays can feel bigger than they really are.
components.chinese: 現在的人已經習慣很多事情幾乎立刻就能得到。他們可以幾分鐘內叫外賣、立刻串流看電影、幾秒鐘內傳訊息，也可以按一下螢幕就找到資訊。當生活長期以這種速度運作時，等待本身就會開始令人覺得不自然。某程度上，科技像是把人訓練成習慣用快轉模式去過生活，所以即使只是一些小小的延遲，也可能讓人覺得被放大了。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: These days, people are used to getting things almost instantly.
They can order food in minutes, stream a film straight away, send a message in
seconds, and search for information at the tap of a screen. When life starts
moving at that speed, waiting begins to feel unnatural. In a way, technology has
trained people to expect life on fast-forward, so even small delays can feel bigger
than they really are.
[現在的人已經習慣很多事情幾乎立刻就能得到。他們可以幾分鐘內叫外賣、立刻串流看電
影、幾秒鐘內傳訊息，也可以按一下螢幕就找到資訊。當生活長期以這種速度運作時，等待
本身就會開始令人覺得不自然。某程度上，科技像是把人訓練成習慣用快轉模式去過生活，
所以即使只是一些小小的延遲，也可能讓人覺得被放大了。]

components.english: For example, if a website loads slowly, if a queue moves too little, or if someone does not reply to a message quickly, many people become irritated almost at once. In the past, they might have shrugged it off, but now their patience can wear thin very quickly.
components.chinese: 例如，如果網站開得慢一點、排隊前進得太慢，或者別人沒有很快回覆訊息，很多人往往幾乎立刻就會感到煩躁。放在以前，他們也許只是聳聳肩就算了；但現在，耐性往往一下子就被磨薄了。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if a website loads slowly, if a queue moves too little, or
if someone does not reply to a message quickly, many people become irritated
almost at once. In the past, they might have shrugged it off, but now their
patience can wear thin very quickly.
[例如，如果網站開得慢一點、排隊前進得太慢，或者別人沒有很快回覆訊息，很多人往往
幾乎立刻就會感到煩躁。放在以前，他們也許只是聳聳肩就算了；但現在，耐性往往一下子
就被磨薄了。]

components.english: So yes, I would say people are generally less patient now, because the world around them keeps whispering that everything should happen quickly.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我會說現在的人普遍比較沒有耐性，因為周圍的世界一直在低聲告訴他們：所有事情都應該快一點發生。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, I would say people are generally less patient now, because
the world around them keeps whispering that everything should happen
quickly.
[所以，是的，我會說現在的人普遍比較沒有耐性，因為周圍的世界一直在低聲告訴他們：
所有事情都應該快一點發生。]

components.english: That said, I do not think people are less patient in every sense, because sometimes modern life also forces them to develop a different kind of patience.
components.chinese: 不過，我也不認為人們在每一個層面上都變得更沒有耐性，因為有時現代生活同樣也逼著他們培養出另一種不同的耐性。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think people are less patient in every sense, because
sometimes modern life also forces them to develop a different kind of patience.
[不過，我也不認為人們在每一個層面上都變得更沒有耐性，因為有時現代生活同樣也逼著
他們培養出另一種不同的耐性。]

components.english: For instance, people today often have to deal with long working hours, career pressure, financial stress, and complicated personal lives. Those things can make them emotionally tougher, even if they are less patient with small everyday inconveniences. In other words, many people have become less patient with slow service, but perhaps more patient with life’s heavier burdens. So the picture is not entirely black and white.
components.chinese: 例如，現在的人常常要面對長工時、事業壓力、經濟負擔，以及複雜的人際生活。這些事情有時反而會令他們在情緒上變得更能扛，即使他們對一些日常小麻煩的耐性變少了。換句話說，很多人也許對慢服務更沒有耐性，但對人生裡較沉重的負擔，反而可能更能忍耐。所以整件事並不是完全非黑即白。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: For instance, people today often have to deal with long
working hours, career pressure, financial stress, and complicated personal lives.
Those things can make them emotionally tougher, even if they are less patient
with small everyday inconveniences. In other words, many people have become
less patient with slow service, but perhaps more patient with life’s heavier
burdens. So the picture is not entirely black and white.
[例如，現在的人常常要面對長工時、事業壓力、經濟負擔，以及複雜的人際生活。這些事情
有時反而會令他們在情緒上變得更能扛，即使他們對一些日常小麻煩的耐性變少了。換句
話說，很多人也許對慢服務更沒有耐性，但對人生裡較沉重的負擔，反而可能更能忍耐。所
以整件事並不是完全非黑即白。]

components.english: For instance, someone may get annoyed if the Wi-Fi is slow for five minutes, but the same person may quietly keep going through years of work stress or family responsibility without giving up. That contrast says quite a lot.
components.chinese: 例如，一個人可能會因為 Wi-Fi 慢了五分鐘而很不耐煩，但同一個人卻可能在多年工作壓力或家庭責任之下，仍然安靜地撐下去而沒有放棄。這種對比本身其實很能說明問題。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, someone may get annoyed if the Wi-Fi is slow for
five minutes, but the same person may quietly keep going through years of work
stress or family responsibility without giving up. That contrast says quite a lot.
[例如，一個人可能會因為 Wi-Fi 慢了五分鐘而很不耐煩，但同一個人卻可能在多年工作壓
力或家庭責任之下，仍然安靜地撐下去而沒有放棄。這種對比本身其實很能說明問題。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say people today are probably less patient with speed, delay, and everyday frustration than people in the past. However, that does not necessarily mean they are weaker; it may simply mean that modern life has shortened their fuse in some areas while hardening them in others.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，和過去相比，現在的人大概在面對速度、延遲，以及日常瑣碎挫折時，的確比較沒有耐性。不過，這未必代表他們比較脆弱；更可能只是現代生活在某些方面縮短了他們的耐性，同時又在另一些方面把他們磨得更堅韌。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say people today are probably less patient with
speed, delay, and everyday frustration than people in the past. However, that
does not necessarily mean they are weaker; it may simply mean that modern life
has shortened their fuse in some areas while hardening them in others.
[總結來說，我會說，和過去相比，現在的人大概在面對速度、延遲，以及日常瑣碎挫折時，
的確比較沒有耐性。不過，這未必代表他們比較脆弱；更可能只是現代生活在某些方面縮短
了他們的耐性，同時又在另一些方面把他們磨得更堅韌。]

question.english: What are the positive and negative effects of waiting on society?
question.chinese: 等待這件事，對社會有甚麼正面和負面影響？

source_lines.text: Topic: What are the positive and negative effects of waiting on society?
等待這件事，對社會有甚麼正面和負面影響？

components.english: I think waiting can have both positive and negative effects on society, depending on whether it creates order or simply creates frustration.
components.chinese: 我認為，等待對社會既可以有正面影響，也可以有負面影響，關鍵在於它究竟是在維持秩序，還是在製造挫敗感。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think waiting can have both positive and negative effects on society,
depending on whether it creates order or simply creates frustration.
[我認為，等待對社會既可以有正面影響，也可以有負面影響，關鍵在於它究竟是在維持秩
序，還是在製造挫敗感。]

components.english: On the positive side, waiting teaches people patience, self-control, and respect for fairness. In daily life, not everyone can be served first, so a willingness to wait helps society function more smoothly. Whether people are queuing for public transport, medical services, or government procedures, waiting can act like a quiet social rule that stops everything from turning into chaos. In that sense, it is not just delay; it is discipline in disguise.
components.chinese: 從正面來看，等待能教人耐性、自制力，以及對公平的尊重。在日常生活中，不可能每個人都排第一，所以願意等待其實能令整個社會運作得更順暢。無論是排隊搭車、看醫生，還是辦理政府手續，等待很多時候都像一條安靜卻重要的社會規則，避免一切亂成一團。從這個角度來看，它不只是延遲，而是一種披著延遲外衣的紀律。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: On the positive side, waiting teaches people patience,
self-control, and respect for fairness. In daily life, not everyone can be served
first, so a willingness to wait helps society function more smoothly. Whether
people are queuing for public transport, medical services, or government
procedures, waiting can act like a quiet social rule that stops everything from
turning into chaos. In that sense, it is not just delay; it is discipline in disguise.
[從正面來看，等待能教人耐性、自制力，以及對公平的尊重。在日常生活中，不可能每個人
都排第一，所以願意等待其實能令整個社會運作得更順暢。無論是排隊搭車、看醫生，還是
辦理政府手續，等待很多時候都像一條安靜卻重要的社會規則，避免一切亂成一團。從這個
角度來看，它不只是延遲，而是一種披著延遲外衣的紀律。]

components.english: For example, in a hospital, if everyone demanded immediate attention regardless of urgency, the system would fall apart. The fact that people wait their turn allows the most serious cases to be handled first, which makes the whole arrangement fairer and more humane.
components.chinese: 例如，在醫院裡，如果每個人都不顧輕重緩急地要求立刻得到處理，整個制度很快就會崩潰。正因為人們願意等候自己的次序，最嚴重的個案才能先被處理，這樣整個安排才會更公平，也更有人性。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, in a hospital, if everyone demanded immediate
attention regardless of urgency, the system would fall apart. The fact that
people wait their turn allows the most serious cases to be handled first, which
makes the whole arrangement fairer and more humane.
[例如，在醫院裡，如果每個人都不顧輕重緩急地要求立刻得到處理，整個制度很快就會崩
潰。正因為人們願意等候自己的次序，最嚴重的個案才能先被處理，這樣整個安排才會更公
平，也更有人性。]

components.english: So, one positive effect of waiting is that it helps society stay orderly, fair, and workable, especially in shared public systems.
components.chinese: 所以，等待其中一個正面影響，就是它能幫助社會在共享的公共制度中維持秩序、公平，以及基本運作。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one positive effect of waiting is that it helps society stay
orderly, fair, and workable, especially in shared public systems.
[所以，等待其中一個正面影響，就是它能幫助社會在共享的公共制度中維持秩序、公平，
以及基本運作。]

components.english: That said, waiting can also have clear negative effects when it becomes excessive, inefficient, or unnecessary.
components.chinese: 不過，當等待變得過長、低效，或者根本沒有必要時，它也會帶來很明顯的負面影響。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, waiting can also have clear negative effects when it becomes
excessive, inefficient, or unnecessary.
[不過，當等待變得過長、低效，或者根本沒有必要時，它也會帶來很明顯的負面影響。]

components.english: If people spend too much time waiting, it can waste energy, damage productivity, and create a great deal of stress. Long queues, delayed services, and endless bureaucracy can feel like sand in the gears of society. Over time, that kind of waiting wears people down. It can make them more impatient, more distrustful, and less satisfied with public life. In other words, a little waiting may build patience, but too much waiting can drain goodwill.
components.chinese: 如果人們花太多時間在等待上，它就可能浪費精力、削弱生產力，並帶來大量壓力。漫長的隊伍、延誤的服務，以及沒完沒了的官僚程序，往往就像卡在社會齒輪裡的沙子。久而久之，這種等待會把人磨得很累，令他們變得更不耐煩、更不信任制度，也對公共生活更不滿。換句話說，一點等待也許能培養耐性，但太多等待卻會耗盡人與人之間、以及人與制度之間的善意。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: If people spend too much time waiting, it can waste energy,
damage productivity, and create a great deal of stress. Long queues, delayed
services, and endless bureaucracy can feel like sand in the gears of society. Over
time, that kind of waiting wears people down. It can make them more
impatient, more distrustful, and less satisfied with public life. In other words, a
little waiting may build patience, but too much waiting can drain goodwill.
[如果人們花太多時間在等待上，它就可能浪費精力、削弱生產力，並帶來大量壓力。漫長
的隊伍、延誤的服務，以及沒完沒了的官僚程序，往往就像卡在社會齒輪裡的沙子。久而久
之，這種等待會把人磨得很累，令他們變得更不耐煩、更不信任制度，也對公共生活更不
滿。換句話說，一點等待也許能培養耐性，但太多等待卻會耗盡人與人之間、以及人與制度
之間的善意。]

components.english: For instance, if commuters waste hours every week waiting for delayed transport, or if citizens have to wait months for basic services, the result is not patience but frustration. In that case, waiting stops being a social virtue and starts becoming a social burden.
components.chinese: 例如，如果上班族每星期都因交通延誤而白白浪費好幾小時，或者市民要等上幾個月才能得到基本服務，最後產生的就不會是耐性，而是挫敗感。在那種情況下，等待就不再是一種社會美德，而開始變成社會負擔。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if commuters waste hours every week waiting for
delayed transport, or if citizens have to wait months for basic services, the result
is not patience but frustration. In that case, waiting stops being a social virtue
and starts becoming a social burden.
[例如，如果上班族每星期都因交通延誤而白白浪費好幾小時，或者市民要等上幾個月才能
得到基本服務，最後產生的就不會是耐性，而是挫敗感。在那種情況下，等待就不再是一種
社會美德，而開始變成社會負擔。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say waiting has a double-edged effect on society. When it protects fairness and order, it can be valuable; when it comes from inefficiency and poor systems, it can waste time, weaken trust, and make society feel much heavier than it needs to be.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說等待對社會有一種雙刃劍式的影響。當它是在保護公平和秩序時，它可以很有價值；但當它來自低效率和糟糕制度時，它就會浪費時間、削弱信任，並令整個社會顯得比它本來應有的更沉重。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say waiting has a double-edged effect on society.
When it protects fairness and order, it can be valuable; when it comes from
inefficiency and poor systems, it can waste time, weaken trust, and make society
feel much heavier than it needs to be.
[總結來說，我會說等待對社會有一種雙刃劍式的影響。當它是在保護公平和秩序時，它可
以很有價值；但當它來自低效率和糟糕制度時，它就會浪費時間、削弱信任，並令整個社會
顯得比它本來應有的更沉重。]

question.english: Are you a patient person?
question.chinese: 你是一個有耐性的人嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Are you a patient person?
你是一個有耐性的人嗎？

components.english: Yes, I’d say I’m a fairly patient person, at least in most everyday situations.
components.chinese: 是的，我會說自己算是一個頗有耐性的人，至少在大多數日常情況下是這樣。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, I’d say I’m a fairly patient person, at least in most everyday
situations.
[是的，我會說自己算是一個頗有耐性的人，至少在大多數日常情況下是這樣。]

components.english: The main reason is that I do not usually see much value in rushing, snapping, or losing my cool too quickly. In my view, patience is a quiet kind of strength. It helps people think more clearly, handle problems more sensibly, and deal with others without making a bad situation worse. I also think many things in life simply take time, so there is no point trying to force everything to happen at once.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，我通常覺得太快急躁、發脾氣，或者一下子就失去冷靜，其實沒有太大好處。在我看來，耐性是一種安靜但有力量的特質。它能幫助人更清楚地思考、更理性地處理問題，也能在面對別人時避免把原本已經不太好的情況弄得更糟。我也認為，人生中很多事情本來就需要時間，所以硬要所有事情一下子發生，其實沒有甚麼意義。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that I do not usually see much value in
rushing, snapping, or losing my cool too quickly. In my view, patience is a quiet
kind of strength. It helps people think more clearly, handle problems more
sensibly, and deal with others without making a bad situation worse. I also
think many things in life simply take time, so there is no point trying to force
everything to happen at once.
[主要原因是，我通常覺得太快急躁、發脾氣，或者一下子就失去冷靜，其實沒有太大好處。
在我看來，耐性是一種安靜但有力量的特質。它能幫助人更清楚地思考、更理性地處理問題
，也能在面對別人時避免把原本已經不太好的情況弄得更糟。我也認為，人生中很多事情本
來就需要時間，所以硬要所有事情一下子發生，其實沒有甚麼意義。]

components.english: For example, if I am working on something difficult, such as learning a new skill or dealing with a slow group project, I usually try to stay calm and take it step by step instead of getting frustrated straight away. The same applies when I am talking to children or older people. I tend to listen first rather than cut in impatiently.
components.chinese: 例如，如果我正在處理一件比較困難的事情，例如學習一項新技能，或者面對一個進度很慢的小組項目，我通常都會先讓自己冷靜下來，一步一步去做，而不是立刻變得很煩躁。同樣地，當我和小朋友或長者相處時，我也通常會先聽對方說，而不是不耐煩地打斷。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if I am working on something difficult, such as
learning a new skill or dealing with a slow group project, I usually try to stay
calm and take it step by step instead of getting frustrated straight away. The
same applies when I am talking to children or older people. I tend to listen first
rather than cut in impatiently.
[例如，如果我正在處理一件比較困難的事情，例如學習一項新技能，或者面對一個進度很
慢的小組項目，我通常都會先讓自己冷靜下來，一步一步去做，而不是立刻變得很煩躁。同
樣地，當我和小朋友或長者相處時，我也通常會先聽對方說，而不是不耐煩地打斷。]

components.english: So overall, yes, I do think I’m a patient person, because I generally prefer staying steady to reacting too quickly.
components.chinese: 所以整體來說，是的，我的確認為自己是一個有耐性的人，因為比起太快作出反應，我通常更傾向先讓自己穩住。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So overall, yes, I do think I’m a patient person, because I
generally prefer staying steady to reacting too quickly.
[所以整體來說，是的，我的確認為自己是一個有耐性的人，因為比起太快作出反應，我通常
更傾向先讓自己穩住。]

components.english: That said, I would not pretend that I’m patient in absolutely every situation.
components.chinese: 不過，我也不會假裝自己在所有情況下都百分之百有耐性。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I would not pretend that I’m patient in absolutely every
situation.
[不過，我也不會假裝自己在所有情況下都百分之百有耐性。]

components.english: To be honest, my patience can wear thin when people are repeatedly careless, rude, or unwilling to take responsibility for things they should clearly handle themselves. Everyone has a limit, and I’m no exception. If the same problem keeps happening again and again, or if someone wastes other people’s time without a good reason, I can start to feel that my patience is being tested.
components.chinese: 老實說，如果別人一而再、再而三地粗心大意、沒有禮貌，或者明明應該為自己的事情負責卻始終不肯面對，我的耐性也會慢慢被磨薄。每個人都有底線，我也不例外。如果同一個問題不停重複出現，或者有人毫無理由地浪費別人的時間，我就會開始覺得自己的耐性正在被考驗。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: To be honest, my patience can wear thin when people are
repeatedly careless, rude, or unwilling to take responsibility for things they
should clearly handle themselves. Everyone has a limit, and I’m no exception. If
the same problem keeps happening again and again, or if someone wastes other
people’s time without a good reason, I can start to feel that my patience is being
tested.
[老實說，如果別人一而再、再而三地粗心大意、沒有禮貌，或者明明應該為自己的事情負責
卻始終不肯面對，我的耐性也會慢慢被磨薄。每個人都有底線，我也不例外。如果同一個問
題不停重複出現，或者有人毫無理由地浪費別人的時間，我就會開始覺得自己的耐性正在
被考驗。]

components.english: For instance, if someone turns up late every single time and still acts as if it is no big deal, I may stay polite on the surface, but inwardly I would probably be quite annoyed. In situations like that, patience starts to feel less like a virtue and more like a test of endurance.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人每一次都遲到，卻仍然表現得好像完全不是一回事，那我表面上也許仍然會保持禮貌，但心裡其實多半已經相當不滿。在那種情況下，耐性有時就不再只是美德，而更像是一場耐力測試。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, if someone turns up late every single time and still
acts as if it is no big deal, I may stay polite on the surface, but inwardly I would
probably be quite annoyed. In situations like that, patience starts to feel less like
a virtue and more like a test of endurance.
[例如，如果一個人每一次都遲到，卻仍然表現得好像完全不是一回事，那我表面上也許仍
然會保持禮貌，但心裡其實多半已經相當不滿。在那種情況下，耐性有時就不再只是美德，
而更像是一場耐力測試。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say I’m patient by nature, but not endlessly so. I can stay calm for quite a long time, but like anyone else, I have my limits, especially when respect and responsibility are missing.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說自己本質上是有耐性的，但那並不是無止境的。我通常可以冷靜很久，不過和任何人一樣，我也有自己的界線，特別是當尊重和責任感缺席的時候。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say I’m patient by nature, but not endlessly so. I can
stay calm for quite a long time, but like anyone else, I have my limits, especially
when respect and responsibility are missing.
[總結來說，我會說自己本質上是有耐性的，但那並不是無止境的。我通常可以冷靜很久，不
過和任何人一樣，我也有自己的界線，特別是當尊重和責任感缺席的時候。]

question.english: Do you think it is possible to teach people to be more patient?
question.chinese: 你認為人是可以被教得更有耐性嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Do you think it is possible to teach people to be more patient?
你認為人是可以被教得更有耐性嗎？

components.english: Yes, I do. I think it is definitely possible to teach people to be more patient, although it usually takes time rather than happening overnight.
components.chinese: 是的，我認為可以。我覺得人絕對是可以被教得更有耐性的，只不過這通常需要時間，而不是一夜之間就做到。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, I do. I think it is definitely possible to teach people to be more
patient, although it usually takes time rather than happening overnight.
[是的，我認為可以。我覺得人絕對是可以被教得更有耐性的，只不過這通常需要時間，而
不是一夜之間就做到。]

components.english: The main reason is that patience is not just a personality trait; it is also a habit and a skill. People can learn to pause, manage frustration, and see the bigger picture instead of reacting on impulse. In that sense, patience is a bit like a muscle: the more people practise it, the stronger it becomes. If someone is always taught that everything must happen instantly, they may become short-tempered; but if they learn how to wait, reflect, and stay calm, they are far more likely to grow into a patient person.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，耐性不只是天生性格的一部分，它同時也是一種習慣和能力。人是可以學會先停一停、處理自己的挫折感，以及看到更大的整體，而不是一有情緒就立刻反應。從這個角度來說，耐性有點像肌肉一樣：越鍛鍊，就越強。如果一個人總是被教成凡事都要立刻有結果，他就可能變得很急躁；但如果他學會等待、反思，以及保持冷靜，他就更有可能慢慢成為一個有耐性的人。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that patience is not just a personality trait; it
is also a habit and a skill. People can learn to pause, manage frustration, and see
the bigger picture instead of reacting on impulse. In that sense, patience is a bit
like a muscle: the more people practise it, the stronger it becomes. If someone is
always taught that everything must happen instantly, they may become
short-tempered; but if they learn how to wait, reflect, and stay calm, they are far
more likely to grow into a patient person.
[主要原因是，耐性不只是天生性格的一部分，它同時也是一種習慣和能力。人是可以學會
先停一停、處理自己的挫折感，以及看到更大的整體，而不是一有情緒就立刻反應。從這個
角度來說，耐性有點像肌肉一樣：越鍛鍊，就越強。如果一個人總是被教成凡事都要立刻有
結果，他就可能變得很急躁；但如果他學會等待、反思，以及保持冷靜，他就更有可能慢慢
成為一個有耐性的人。]

components.english: For example, parents and teachers can help children become more patient by teaching them to take turns, finish tasks step by step, or save money for something they really want instead of getting it straight away. Through small experiences like that, children begin to understand that not everything worth having comes quickly.
components.chinese: 例如，父母和老師可以透過教孩子輪流、一步一步完成事情，或者為自己真正想要的東西儲錢，而不是立刻得到它，來幫助孩子變得更有耐性。透過這些細小的經歷，孩子會慢慢明白：不是所有值得擁有的東西，都會很快來到。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, parents and teachers can help children become more
patient by teaching them to take turns, finish tasks step by step, or save money
for something they really want instead of getting it straight away. Through
small experiences like that, children begin to understand that not everything
worth having comes quickly.
[例如，父母和老師可以透過教孩子輪流、一步一步完成事情，或者為自己真正想要的東西
儲錢，而不是立刻得到它，來幫助孩子變得更有耐性。透過這些細小的經歷，孩子會慢慢明
白：不是所有值得擁有的東西，都會很快來到。]

components.english: So yes, I would say patience can definitely be taught, because people can be trained to slow down, handle frustration better, and become more emotionally steady over time.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我會說耐性絕對是可以被教出來的，因為人是可以被訓練得慢下來、更好地面對挫折，並隨著時間變得更穩定的。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, I would say patience can definitely be taught, because
people can be trained to slow down, handle frustration better, and become more
emotionally steady over time.
[所以，是的，我會說耐性絕對是可以被教出來的，因為人是可以被訓練得慢下來、更好地
面對挫折，並隨著時間變得更穩定的。]

components.english: That said, I do not think teaching patience means every person will become calm and patient in exactly the same way.
components.chinese: 不過，我並不認為教耐性就等於每一個人最後都會用完全一樣的方式變得冷靜和有耐性。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think teaching patience means every person will
become calm and patient in exactly the same way.
[不過，我並不認為教耐性就等於每一個人最後都會用完全一樣的方式變得冷靜和有耐

components.english: Personality still matters, and so do stress, tiredness, and life circumstances. Some people are naturally more hot-headed, while others are more even-tempered to begin with. On top of that, when someone is under constant pressure, even a patient person can wear thin. So I would say patience can be nurtured, but it cannot simply be switched on like a light.
components.chinese: 性格始終有影響，而壓力、疲累，以及生活處境也同樣重要。有些人天生比較急、火氣比較大；也有些人本來就比較平和。除此之外，當一個人長期承受壓力時，即使本來很有耐性，也可能會被磨得越來越薄。所以，我會說耐性是可以被培養的，但它並不能像開燈那樣一下子就打開。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: Personality still matters, and so do stress, tiredness, and life
circumstances. Some people are naturally more hot-headed, while others are
more even-tempered to begin with. On top of that, when someone is under
constant pressure, even a patient person can wear thin. So I would say patience
can be nurtured, but it cannot simply be switched on like a light.
[性格始終有影響，而壓力、疲累，以及生活處境也同樣重要。有些人天生比較急、火氣比較
大；也有些人本來就比較平和。除此之外，當一個人長期承受壓力時，即使本來很有耐性，
也可能會被磨得越來越薄。所以，我會說耐性是可以被培養的，但它並不能像開燈那樣一下
子就打開。]

components.english: For instance, an overworked parent may know perfectly well that they should stay calm, but after a long exhausting day, they may still snap at something small. That does not mean patience cannot be taught; it just means human beings are not machines.
components.chinese: 例如，一位工作過度疲累的家長，其實完全知道自己應該保持冷靜，但在漫長又耗神的一天之後，他仍然可能會因為一件小事而失去耐性。這並不代表耐性不能被教會，只是說明人終究不是機器。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, an overworked parent may know perfectly well that
they should stay calm, but after a long exhausting day, they may still snap at
something small. That does not mean patience cannot be taught; it just means
human beings are not machines.
[例如，一位工作過度疲累的家長，其實完全知道自己應該保持冷靜，但在漫長又耗神的一
天之後，他仍然可能會因為一件小事而失去耐性。這並不代表耐性不能被教會，只是說明人
終究不是機器。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say it is absolutely possible to teach people to be more patient, but the process takes practice, guidance, and the right environment. In the end, patience is more like a seed that has to be nurtured over time than something that can simply be planted in a person overnight.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說人絕對可以被教得更有耐性，但這個過程需要練習、引導，以及合適的環境。說到底，耐性更像是一粒需要慢慢栽培的種子，而不是一種可以一夜之間灌輸進去的東西

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say it is absolutely possible to teach people to be
more patient, but the process takes practice, guidance, and the right
environment. In the end, patience is more like a seed that has to be nurtured
over time than something that can simply be planted in a person overnight.
[總結來說，我會說人絕對可以被教得更有耐性，但這個過程需要練習、引導，以及合適的環
境。說到底，耐性更像是一粒需要慢慢栽培的種子，而不是一種可以一夜之間灌輸進去的東
西

question.english: Some people believe that impatience helps the development of technology. Do you agree with that? Why do you think so?
question.chinese: 有些人認為，不耐煩有助於科技發展。你同意嗎？為甚麼？

source_lines.text: Topic: Some people believe that impatience helps the development of
technology. Do you agree with that? Why do you think so?
有些人認為，不耐煩有助於科技發展。你同意嗎？為甚麼？

components.english: To some extent, yes, I do agree that impatience can help the development of technology.
components.chinese: 某程度上，是的，我的確認為不耐煩可以推動科技發展。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: To some extent, yes, I do agree that impatience can help the development
of technology.
[某程度上，是的，我的確認為不耐煩可以推動科技發展。]

components.english: The main reason is that impatience often comes from frustration with inconvenience, delay, or inefficiency. When people get tired of waiting, repeating the same task, or doing things the hard way, they start looking for faster and smarter solutions. In that sense, impatience can be the spark that lights the fire of innovation. It pushes people to ask, “Why does this still take so long?” and then try to build something better.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，不耐煩很多時候來自對不方便、延誤，或者低效率的不滿。當人們厭倦了等待、厭倦了重複做同一件事，或者厭倦了用很麻煩的方法處理事情，他們就會開始尋找更快、更聰明的解決方法。從這個角度來看，不耐煩可以成為點燃創新的那一點火花。它會推動人們去問：「為甚麼這件事還要花這麼久？」然後開始嘗試造出更好的東西。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that impatience often comes from
frustration with inconvenience, delay, or inefficiency. When people get tired of
waiting, repeating the same task, or doing things the hard way, they start
looking for faster and smarter solutions. In that sense, impatience can be the
spark that lights the fire of innovation. It pushes people to ask, “Why does this
still take so long?” and then try to build something better.
[主要原因是，不耐煩很多時候來自對不方便、延誤，或者低效率的不滿。當人們厭倦了等
待、厭倦了重複做同一件事，或者厭倦了用很麻煩的方法處理事情，他們就會開始尋找更
快、更聰明的解決方法。從這個角度來看，不耐煩可以成為點燃創新的那一點火花。它會推
動人們去問：「為甚麼這件事還要花這麼久？」然後開始嘗試造出更好的東西。]

components.english: For example, people did not want to queue at banks, so online banking and digital payments became more important. In the same way, people hated waiting for taxis or takeaways, so apps were developed to make those services faster and easier. A lot of useful technology is really built on the simple human desire to save time and effort.
components.chinese: 例如，人們不想在銀行排長龍，所以網上銀行和電子支付就變得愈來愈重要。同樣地，人們討厭等的士，也不想苦苦等外賣，所以各種應用程式就被發展出來，令這些服務更快、更方便。很多有用的科技，其實正正是建立在人類想節省時間和力氣這種很直接的渴望之上的。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, people did not want to queue at banks, so online
banking and digital payments became more important. In the same way, people
hated waiting for taxis or takeaways, so apps were developed to make those
services faster and easier. A lot of useful technology is really built on the simple
human desire to save time and effort.
[例如，人們不想在銀行排長龍，所以網上銀行和電子支付就變得愈來愈重要。同樣地，人
們討厭等的士，也不想苦苦等外賣，所以各種應用程式就被發展出來，令這些服務更快、更
方便。很多有用的科技，其實正正是建立在人類想節省時間和力氣這種很直接的渴望之上

components.english: So yes, I would say impatience can be a powerful driver of technological progress, because it makes people unwilling to settle for slow or clumsy systems.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我會說不耐煩可以成為科技進步的一股強大動力，因為它會令人不甘心一直接受那些緩慢而笨拙的系統。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, I would say impatience can be a powerful driver of
technological progress, because it makes people unwilling to settle for slow or
clumsy systems.
[所以，是的，我會說不耐煩可以成為科技進步的一股強大動力，因為它會令人不甘心一直
接受那些緩慢而笨拙的系統。]

components.english: That said, I would not say impatience is always a good thing in technology.
components.chinese: 不過，我也不會說不耐煩在科技發展中永遠都是好事。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I would not say impatience is always a good thing in
technology.
[不過，我也不會說不耐煩在科技發展中永遠都是好事。]

components.english: If impatience turns into a rush to produce results at any cost, it can do more harm than good. Good technology needs not only speed, but also care, testing, and responsibility. Otherwise, people may create products that are fast but flawed, exciting but unsafe. In other words, impatience may start the engine, but patience is what keeps the machine from falling apart.
components.chinese: 如果不耐煩變成一種不惜一切也要趕快出結果的衝動，那它就可能弊多於利。好的科技不只需要速度，還需要細心、測試，以及責任感。否則，人們做出來的產品就可能只是快，卻不夠完善；只是吸引，卻不夠安全。換句話說，不耐煩也許能發動引擎，但真正令整部機器不會散掉的，還是耐性。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: If impatience turns into a rush to produce results at any cost,
it can do more harm than good. Good technology needs not only speed, but also
care, testing, and responsibility. Otherwise, people may create products that are
fast but flawed, exciting but unsafe. In other words, impatience may start the
engine, but patience is what keeps the machine from falling apart.
[如果不耐煩變成一種不惜一切也要趕快出結果的衝動，那它就可能弊多於利。好的科技不
只需要速度，還需要細心、測試，以及責任感。否則，人們做出來的產品就可能只是快，卻不
夠完善；只是吸引，卻不夠安全。換句話說，不耐煩也許能發動引擎，但真正令整部機器不
會散掉的，還是耐性。]

components.english: For instance, if a company releases a new app, device, or AI tool too quickly, it may be full of bugs, invade people’s privacy, or create problems nobody thought through properly. In serious areas such as medicine or transport, rushing technology can be especially risky.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一家公司太快推出新的應用程式、裝置，或者人工智能工具，那它就可能充滿漏洞、侵犯私隱，或者製造出一些根本沒有被好好思考過的問題。在醫療或交通這些較嚴肅的範疇裡，過於倉促地發展科技，風險尤其大。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if a company releases a new app, device, or AI tool too
quickly, it may be full of bugs, invade people’s privacy, or create problems
nobody thought through properly. In serious areas such as medicine or
transport, rushing technology can be especially risky.
[例如，如果一家公司太快推出新的應用程式、裝置，或者人工智能工具，那它就可能充滿
漏洞、侵犯私隱，或者製造出一些根本沒有被好好思考過的問題。在醫療或交通這些較嚴肅
的範疇裡，過於倉促地發展科技，風險尤其大。]

components.english: Overall, I partly agree. Impatience often helps technology move forward by pushing people to solve problems faster, but by itself it is not enough. The best progress usually happens when people are impatient with the problem, but patient with the solution.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我是部分同意的。不耐煩很多時候的確會推動科技向前，因為它逼使人們更快去解決問題；但單靠不耐煩本身並不足夠。最好的進步，通常發生在這樣的情況下：人們對問題不耐煩，卻對解決方法保持耐性。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, I partly agree. Impatience often helps technology move
forward by pushing people to solve problems faster, but by itself it is not
enough. The best progress usually happens when people are impatient with the
problem, but patient with the solution.
[總結來說，我是部分同意的。不耐煩很多時候的確會推動科技向前，因為它逼使人們更快
去解決問題；但單靠不耐煩本身並不足夠。最好的進步，通常發生在這樣的情況下：人們對
問題不耐煩，卻對解決方法保持耐性。]

question.english: Where do you go to get good advice?
question.chinese: 你通常會去找誰，或者去甚麼地方尋求好的建議？

source_lines.text: Topic: Where do you go to get good advice?
你通常會去找誰，或者去甚麼地方尋求好的建議？

components.english: Usually, I go first to people I trust, especially close family members or a really good friend.
components.chinese: 通常來說，我第一個會去找我信任的人，特別是親近的家人或者很好的朋友。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Usually, I go first to people I trust, especially close family members or a
really good friend.
[通常來說，我第一個會去找我信任的人，特別是親近的家人或者很好的朋友。]

components.english: The main reason is that good advice is not just about sounding wise; it has to fit the person as well. People who know me well understand my personality, my habits, and even my blind spots. So when I am confused, they can often tell whether I am overthinking, being too emotional, or missing something obvious. In that sense, good advice often begins with good understanding.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，好的建議不只是聽起來有道理而已；它還要適合那個人本身。真正了解我的人，會知道我的性格、習慣，甚至我容易忽略的盲點。所以，當我感到困惑時，他們往往能看出我是不是想太多、太受情緒影響，或者漏看了某些很明顯的事情。從這個角度來說，好的建議很多時都是從真正的了解開始的。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that good advice is not just about sounding
wise; it has to fit the person as well. People who know me well understand my
personality, my habits, and even my blind spots. So when I am confused, they
can often tell whether I am overthinking, being too emotional, or missing
something obvious. In that sense, good advice often begins with good
understanding.
[主要原因是，好的建議不只是聽起來有道理而已；它還要適合那個人本身。真正了解我的
人，會知道我的性格、習慣，甚至我容易忽略的盲點。所以，當我感到困惑時，他們往往能看
出我是不是想太多、太受情緒影響，或者漏看了某些很明顯的事情。從這個角度來說，好的
建議很多時都是從真正的了解開始的。]

components.english: For example, if I am struggling with a personal decision, like whether to change jobs or how to deal with a difficult relationship, I would probably talk to my parents or a close friend first. They may not have the perfect answer, but they usually help me see the situation more clearly.
components.chinese: 例如，如果我正在為一個很個人的決定而煩惱，例如要不要轉工，或者怎樣處理一段困難的人際關係，我大概會先找父母或者親密朋友談一談。他們未必有完美答案，但通常都能幫我把事情看得更清楚。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if I am struggling with a personal decision, like
whether to change jobs or how to deal with a difficult relationship, I would
probably talk to my parents or a close friend first. They may not have the perfect
answer, but they usually help me see the situation more clearly.
[例如，如果我正在為一個很個人的決定而煩惱，例如要不要轉工，或者怎樣處理一段困難
的人際關係，我大概會先找父母或者親密朋友談一談。他們未必有完美答案，但通常都能幫
我把事情看得更清楚。]

components.english: So, for personal matters, I would say the best place to get advice is usually from people who know me well and genuinely want the best for me.
components.chinese: 所以，如果是個人方面的事情，我會說最好的建議來源，通常是那些真正了解我，而且真心希望我好的人。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, for personal matters, I would say the best place to get advice
is usually from people who know me well and genuinely want the best for me.
[所以，如果是個人方面的事情，我會說最好的建議來源，通常是那些真正了解我，而且真
心希望我好的人。]

components.english: That said, if the issue is more serious, technical, or specialised, I would go to someone with real experience or professional knowledge.
components.chinese: 不過，如果問題比較嚴重、比較專門，或者牽涉某些專業層面，我就會去找真正有經驗或有專業知識的人。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, if the issue is more serious, technical, or specialised, I would
go to someone with real experience or professional knowledge.
[不過，如果問題比較嚴重、比較專門，或者牽涉某些專業層面，我就會去找真正有經驗或
有專業知識的人。]

components.english: Sometimes kindness is not enough; you also need accuracy. A caring friend can calm you down, but a teacher, mentor, doctor, or financial expert may be better placed to guide you properly. To me, the key is matching the advice to the problem. You do not ask everyone for everything. Otherwise, you can end up with a lot of opinions and very little clarity.
components.chinese: 有時候，單靠關心是不夠的；你還需要準確的判斷。一位關心你的朋友也許能安撫你，但老師、前輩、醫生，或者理財專家，很多時會更適合提供真正有根據的指引。對我來說，最重要的是讓建議來源和問題本身配對。你不會向每一個人問所有事情，否則最後很可能只得到一大堆意見，卻沒有多少真正的清晰感。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: Sometimes kindness is not enough; you also need accuracy. A
caring friend can calm you down, but a teacher, mentor, doctor, or financial
expert may be better placed to guide you properly. To me, the key is matching
the advice to the problem. You do not ask everyone for everything. Otherwise,
you can end up with a lot of opinions and very little clarity.
[有時候，單靠關心是不夠的；你還需要準確的判斷。一位關心你的朋友也許能安撫你，但
老師、前輩、醫生，或者理財專家，很多時會更適合提供真正有根據的指引。對我來說，最重
要的是讓建議來源和問題本身配對。你不會向每一個人問所有事情，否則最後很可能只得
到一大堆意見，卻沒有多少真正的清晰感。]

components.english: For instance, if I needed advice about my career, I might talk to a mentor or lecturer. If it was about health, I would rather ask a doctor than rely on random opinions. In that case, experience is worth its weight in gold.
components.chinese: 例如，如果我需要的是職業方面的建議，我可能會去問前輩或老師；如果是健康問題，我寧願問醫生，也不會隨便相信雜七雜八的意見。在那種情況下，真正的經驗和專業就非常珍貴。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if I needed advice about my career, I might talk to a
mentor or lecturer. If it was about health, I would rather ask a doctor than rely
on random opinions. In that case, experience is worth its weight in gold.
[例如，如果我需要的是職業方面的建議，我可能會去問前輩或老師；如果是健康問題，我
寧願問醫生，也不會隨便相信雜七雜八的意見。在那種情況下，真正的經驗和專業就非常珍

components.english: Overall, I’d say I usually go to trusted people for emotional perspective and to experienced people for practical guidance. The best advice, in my view, often comes when warmth and wisdom go hand in hand.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，我通常會向信任的人尋求情感上的角度，也會向有經驗的人尋求實際上的指引。在我看來，最好的建議，很多時候都是溫度和智慧並肩同行時出現的。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say I usually go to trusted people for emotional
perspective and to experienced people for practical guidance. The best advice, in
my view, often comes when warmth and wisdom go hand in hand.
[總結來說，我會說，我通常會向信任的人尋求情感上的角度，也會向有經驗的人尋求實際
上的指引。在我看來，最好的建議，很多時候都是溫度和智慧並肩同行時出現的。]

question.english: Whose advice do you follow more, your parents’ or your friends’ advice?
question.chinese: 你通常比較會聽誰的建議，父母還是朋友？

source_lines.text: Topic: Whose advice do you follow more, your parents’ or your friends’ advice?
[你通常比較會聽誰的建議，父母還是朋友？]

components.english: Personally, I would say I tend to follow my parents’ advice more, especially when the decision is important or likely to affect my future.
components.chinese: 就我個人而言，我會說自己通常比較會聽父母的建議，尤其是當那個決定很重要，或者很可能影響我的未來時。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Personally, I would say I tend to follow my parents’ advice more,
especially when the decision is important or likely to affect my future.
[就我個人而言，我會說自己通常比較會聽父母的建議，尤其是當那個決定很重要，或者很
可能影響我的未來時。]

components.english: The main reason is that parents usually have more life experience and, in most cases, genuinely have their children’s best interests at heart. They tend to look at things from a longer-term point of view, so their advice is often steadier and less driven by impulse. Even if I do not agree with them straight away, I still feel their words usually come from care rather than convenience. In that sense, parents may not always tell you what you want to hear, but they often tell you what you need to hear.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，父母通常有人生經驗，而且在大多數情況下，他們的確是真心為孩子着想。他們往往會從較長遠的角度去看事情，所以他們的建議通常更穩，也較少受一時衝動影響。即使我未必會立刻同意他們，我仍然會覺得他們的話通常是出於關心，而不是出於方便自己。從這個角度來說，父母未必總是告訴你想聽的話，但他們往往是在說你需要聽的話。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that parents usually have more life
experience and, in most cases, genuinely have their children’s best interests at
heart. They tend to look at things from a longer-term point of view, so their
advice is often steadier and less driven by impulse. Even if I do not agree with
them straight away, I still feel their words usually come from care rather than
convenience. In that sense, parents may not always tell you what you want to
hear, but they often tell you what you need to hear.
[主要原因是，父母通常有人生經驗，而且在大多數情況下，他們的確是真心為孩子着想。
他們往往會從較長遠的角度去看事情，所以他們的建議通常更穩，也較少受一時衝動影響。
即使我未必會立刻同意他們，我仍然會覺得他們的話通常是出於關心，而不是出於方便自
己。從這個角度來說，父母未必總是告訴你想聽的話，但他們往往是在說你需要聽的話。]

components.english: For example, if I were making a decision about study, work, or money, I would probably take my parents’ opinion more seriously. They may see risks or consequences that I might miss, simply because they have been around the block a few more times than I have.
components.chinese: 例如，如果我要在學業、工作，或者金錢方面作決定，我大概會更認真看待父母的意見。他們可能會看見一些我忽略了的風險或後果，單純因為他們比我多走過幾段路。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if I were making a decision about study, work, or
money, I would probably take my parents’ opinion more seriously. They may see
risks or consequences that I might miss, simply because they have been around
the block a few more times than I have.
[例如，如果我要在學業、工作，或者金錢方面作決定，我大概會更認真看待父母的意見。他
們可能會看見一些我忽略了的風險或後果，單純因為他們比我多走過幾段路。]

components.english: So overall, I follow my parents’ advice more when the issue is serious, because their perspective is usually broader and more grounded.
components.chinese: 所以，整體來說，當事情比較重大時，我會更傾向聽父母的建議，因為他們的角度通常更廣，也更踏實。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So overall, I follow my parents’ advice more when the issue is
serious, because their perspective is usually broader and more grounded.
[所以，整體來說，當事情比較重大時，我會更傾向聽父母的建議，因為他們的角度通常更
廣，也更踏實。]

components.english: That said, I do think friends’ advice can be very valuable too, especially in situations they understand more directly.
components.chinese: 不過，我也認為朋友的建議同樣可以很有價值，尤其是當事情是他們更能直接理解的時候。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do think friends’ advice can be very valuable too, especially
in situations they understand more directly.
[不過，我也認為朋友的建議同樣可以很有價值，尤其是當事情是他們更能直接理解的時

components.english: Friends are often closer to my age, my daily life, and the kind of pressures I am dealing with right now, so sometimes they are more on the same wavelength. They may understand modern trends, social situations, or emotional struggles in a way that feels more immediate. If I need advice about friendships, university life, or something happening in the moment, I might actually turn to my friends first. In other words, parents often give the long view, while friends can offer the close-up shot.
components.chinese: 朋友通常和我年紀比較接近，也更貼近我的日常生活，以及我此刻正在面對的壓力，所以有時他們和我會更在同一個頻道上。他們可能會以一種更即時的方式去理解現代潮流、社交情境，或者情緒上的掙扎。如果我需要的是關於友情、大學生活，或者某些眼前問題的建議，我反而可能會先去找朋友。換句話說，父母往往給的是長鏡頭，而朋友提供的則比較像近鏡。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: Friends are often closer to my age, my daily life, and the kind
of pressures I am dealing with right now, so sometimes they are more on the
same wavelength. They may understand modern trends, social situations, or
emotional struggles in a way that feels more immediate. If I need advice about
friendships, university life, or something happening in the moment, I might
actually turn to my friends first. In other words, parents often give the long
view, while friends can offer the close-up shot.
[朋友通常和我年紀比較接近，也更貼近我的日常生活，以及我此刻正在面對的壓力，所以
有時他們和我會更在同一個頻道上。他們可能會以一種更即時的方式去理解現代潮流、社
交情境，或者情緒上的掙扎。如果我需要的是關於友情、大學生活，或者某些眼前問題的建
議，我反而可能會先去找朋友。換句話說，父母往往給的是長鏡頭，而朋友提供的則比較像
近鏡。]

components.english: For instance, if I had a conflict with a friend or felt stressed about something happening in my social life, I might talk to a close friend before talking to my parents. A friend who is living through a similar stage of life may understand the emotional texture of the situation more naturally.
components.chinese: 例如，如果我和朋友之間有矛盾，或者我正因為社交生活中的某些事而感到壓力，我可能會先和親密朋友談，再和父母談。一位正處於相似人生階段的朋友，往往會更自然地理解那種情況背後的情緒層次。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if I had a conflict with a friend or felt stressed about
something happening in my social life, I might talk to a close friend before
talking to my parents. A friend who is living through a similar stage of life may
understand the emotional texture of the situation more naturally.
[例如，如果我和朋友之間有矛盾，或者我正因為社交生活中的某些事而感到壓力，我可能
會先和親密朋友談，再和父母談。一位正處於相似人生階段的朋友，往往會更自然地理解那
種情況背後的情緒層次。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say I follow my parents’ advice more in big decisions, but I still value my friends’ advice in day-to-day matters. If I had to put it simply, my parents often help me see the bigger picture, while my friends help me deal with the picture I am standing in right now.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說在重大決定上，我通常更傾向聽父母的建議，但在日常事情上，我同樣重視朋友的意見。如果要簡單地說，父母往往幫我看見更大的整體圖畫，而朋友則幫我處理我此刻正站在其中的那幅畫。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, I’d say I follow my parents’ advice more in big decisions,
but I still value my friends’ advice in day-to-day matters. If I had to put it
simply, my parents often help me see the bigger picture, while my friends help
me deal with the picture I am standing in right now.
[總結來說，我會說在重大決定上，我通常更傾向聽父母的建議，但在日常事情上，我同樣
重視朋友的意見。如果要簡單地說，父母往往幫我看見更大的整體圖畫，而朋友則幫我處理
我此刻正站在其中的那幅畫。]

question.english: Have your parents given you much advice?
question.chinese: 你父母有給你很多意見嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Have your parents given you much advice?
你父母有給你很多意見嗎？

components.english: Yes, definitely. My parents have given me a great deal of advice over the years, especially about how to treat people, how to handle responsibility, and how to make sensible decisions.
components.chinese: 是的，當然有。這些年來，我父母給了我很多意見，尤其是在怎樣待人、怎樣承擔責任，以及怎樣作出理智決定這幾方面。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, definitely. My parents have given me a great deal of advice over the
years, especially about how to treat people, how to handle responsibility, and
how to make sensible decisions.
[是的，當然有。這些年來，我父母給了我很多意見，尤其是在怎樣待人、怎樣承擔責任，以
及怎樣作出理智決定這幾方面。]

components.english: To be honest, some of their advice used to sound a bit repetitive when I was younger, because parents often come back to the same points again and again. But now I can see why. A lot of it was not just random nagging; it was their way of trying to keep me on the right track. They often talked about things like being polite, keeping my word, working hard, and not doing anything I would regret later. In a way, their advice was like a steady hand on the wheel.
components.chinese: 老實說，我年紀較小的時候，會覺得他們有些意見聽起來有點重複，因為父母常常會一而再、再而三地說同一些道理。不過現在回頭看，我能明白原因了。很多時那並不是隨便嘮叨，而是他們想把我帶回正軌的一種方式。他們常常提醒我的，通常是要有禮貌、要守承諾、要努力做事，以及不要做一些將來會令自己後悔的事。某程度上，他們的意見就像一隻穩穩放在方向盤上的手。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: To be honest, some of their advice used to sound a bit repetitive
when I was younger, because parents often come back to the same points again
and again. But now I can see why. A lot of it was not just random nagging; it
was their way of trying to keep me on the right track. They often talked about
things like being polite, keeping my word, working hard, and not doing
anything I would regret later. In a way, their advice was like a steady hand on
the wheel.
[老實說，我年紀較小的時候，會覺得他們有些意見聽起來有點重複，因為父母常常會一而
再、再而三地說同一些道理。不過現在回頭看，我能明白原因了。很多時那並不是隨便嘮叨
，而是他們想把我帶回正軌的一種方式。他們常常提醒我的，通常是要有禮貌、要守承諾、
要努力做事，以及不要做一些將來會令自己後悔的事。某程度上，他們的意見就像一隻穩穩
放在方向盤上的手。]

components.english: For example, my parents often told me not to burn bridges, because the way you treat people can come back to you later in life. At the time, I thought that sounded a bit old-fashioned, but the older I get, the more I realise it is actually very practical advice. Relationships, reputation, and trust really do matter.
components.chinese: 例如，我父母常常提醒我，不要把關係弄得太絕，因為你怎樣對待別人，往往會在日後回到自己身上。那時候我覺得這句話有點老派，但年紀愈大，我愈發現那其實是非常實際的忠告。人際關係、個人名聲，以及信任，的確都很重要。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, my parents often told me not to burn bridges, because
the way you treat people can come back to you later in life. At the time, I
thought that sounded a bit old-fashioned, but the older I get, the more I realise
it is actually very practical advice. Relationships, reputation, and trust really do
matter.
[例如，我父母常常提醒我，不要把關係弄得太絕，因為你怎樣對待別人，往往會在日後回
到自己身上。那時候我覺得這句話有點老派，但年紀愈大，我愈發現那其實是非常實際的忠
告。人際關係、個人名聲，以及信任，的確都很重要。]

components.english: So yes, my parents have given me a lot of advice, and although I did not always appreciate it at the time, I think much of it has stayed with me.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我父母的確給了我很多意見。雖然我當時未必每一句都懂得珍惜，但我覺得其中很多話其實一直都留在我心裡。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, my parents have given me a lot of advice, and although I
did not always appreciate it at the time, I think much of it has stayed with me.
[所以，是的，我父母的確給了我很多意見。雖然我當時未必每一句都懂得珍惜，但我覺得
其中很多話其實一直都留在我心裡。]

components.english: That said, I do not think I followed all of their advice immediately, because some lessons only really sink in with age.
components.chinese: 不過，我也不覺得自己當下就有乖乖照做他們所有的意見，因為有些道理真的要隨著年紀增長，才會慢慢聽進去。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think I followed all of their advice immediately,
because some lessons only really sink in with age.
[不過，我也不覺得自己當下就有乖乖照做他們所有的意見，因為有些道理真的要隨著年紀
增長，才會慢慢聽進去。]

components.english: When people are young, they often want to figure things out for themselves, and I was no different. Sometimes advice goes in one ear and out the other, especially when it sounds too familiar. But life has a way of bringing old words back with new meaning. Once you make a few mistakes of your own, advice that once felt like background noise can suddenly sound like wisdom.
components.chinese: 人在年輕的時候，通常都想靠自己去摸索，我也不例外。有時候，那些意見真的會左耳入、右耳出，尤其當它們聽起來太熟悉、太常聽見的時候。不過，人生往往有辦法讓一些舊話在後來重新產生新的意思。當你自己犯過一些錯之後，那些以前聽起來像背景雜音的提醒，突然就會變得像智慧。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: When people are young, they often want to figure things out
for themselves, and I was no different. Sometimes advice goes in one ear and out
the other, especially when it sounds too familiar. But life has a way of bringing
old words back with new meaning. Once you make a few mistakes of your own,
advice that once felt like background noise can suddenly sound like wisdom.
[人在年輕的時候，通常都想靠自己去摸索，我也不例外。有時候，那些意見真的會左耳入、
右耳出，尤其當它們聽起來太熟悉、太常聽見的時候。不過，人生往往有辦法讓一些舊話在
後來重新產生新的意思。當你自己犯過一些錯之後，那些以前聽起來像背景雜音的提醒，突
然就會變得像智慧。]

components.english: For instance, my parents used to tell me to save money and think ahead instead of spending everything on the spot. I did not always take that seriously when I was younger, but later I realised they were absolutely right. A bit of planning can save a lot of stress down the line.
components.chinese: 例如，我父母以前常常叫我要儲錢、要想遠一點，不要有多少就立刻花多少。我年輕時未必真的很認真看待這種話，但後來我發現他們真的說得很對。提前多想一步，往往能省下很多之後的壓力。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, my parents used to tell me to save money and think
ahead instead of spending everything on the spot. I did not always take that
seriously when I was younger, but later I realised they were absolutely right. A
bit of planning can save a lot of stress down the line.
[例如，我父母以前常常叫我要儲錢、要想遠一點，不要有多少就立刻花多少。我年輕時未
必真的很認真看待這種話，但後來我發現他們真的說得很對。提前多想一步，往往能省下很
多之後的壓力。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say my parents have given me plenty of advice, and a lot of it has become more valuable with time. Some advice sounds ordinary when you first hear it, but later it turns out to be the kind of thing that quietly steadies your life.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說我父母給過我很多意見，而其中很多話都是隨著時間過去才愈來愈顯得珍貴。有些忠告在剛聽見時看似很普通，但到後來才會發現，它其實正是那種能安安靜靜把你人生撐穩的東西。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say my parents have given me plenty of advice, and
a lot of it has become more valuable with time. Some advice sounds ordinary
when you first hear it, but later it turns out to be the kind of thing that quietly
steadies your life.
[總結來說，我會說我父母給過我很多意見，而其中很多話都是隨著時間過去才愈來愈顯得
珍貴。有些忠告在剛聽見時看似很普通，但到後來才會發現，它其實正是那種能安安靜靜把
你人生撐穩的東西。]

question.english: What kind of advice do parents give their children?
question.chinese: 父母通常會給孩子甚麼樣的建議？

source_lines.text: Topic: What kind of advice do parents give their children?
父母通常會給孩子甚麼樣的建議？

components.english: I think parents usually give their children two main kinds of advice: practical advice for life, and moral advice about how to behave.
components.chinese: 我認為，父母通常會給孩子兩大類建議：一類是關於生活的實際建議，另一類則是關於做人處事的道德建議。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think parents usually give their children two main kinds of advice:
practical advice for life, and moral advice about how to behave.
[我認為，父母通常會給孩子兩大類建議：一類是關於生活的實際建議，另一類則是關於做
人處事的道德建議。]

components.english: On the practical side, parents often tell children to study hard, manage their time well, stay safe, and make sensible decisions. The reason is quite simple: parents want to protect their children from avoidable mistakes and help them build a more stable future. In that sense, a lot of parental advice is really about trying to give children a map before they step into the wider world.
components.chinese: 在實際層面上，父母常常會叫孩子努力讀書、好好管理時間、注意安全，以及作出明智的決定。原因其實很簡單：父母想保護孩子，避免他們犯下一些本來可以避免的錯誤，同時也希望幫助他們建立一個較穩定的未來。從這個角度來看，很多父母的建議，其實就是在孩子真正踏入更大的世界之前，先給他們一張地圖。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: On the practical side, parents often tell children to study hard,
manage their time well, stay safe, and make sensible decisions. The reason is
quite simple: parents want to protect their children from avoidable mistakes and
help them build a more stable future. In that sense, a lot of parental advice is
really about trying to give children a map before they step into the wider world.
[在實際層面上，父母常常會叫孩子努力讀書、好好管理時間、注意安全，以及作出明智的
決定。原因其實很簡單：父母想保護孩子，避免他們犯下一些本來可以避免的錯誤，同時也
希望幫助他們建立一個較穩定的未來。從這個角度來看，很多父母的建議，其實就是在孩子
真正踏入更大的世界之前，先給他們一張地圖。]

components.english: For example, parents may remind their children to choose their friends carefully, not to waste money, and to think twice before doing something risky. They may also encourage them to work hard at school so that more doors stay open later in life.
components.chinese: 例如，父母可能會提醒孩子要小心選朋友、不要亂花錢，以及在做一些冒險的事情之前要三思而後行。他們也可能會鼓勵孩子在學校裡努力一點，這樣將來人生的路才會有更多選擇。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, parents may remind their children to choose their
friends carefully, not to waste money, and to think twice before doing
something risky. They may also encourage them to work hard at school so that
more doors stay open later in life.
[例如，父母可能會提醒孩子要小心選朋友、不要亂花錢，以及在做一些冒險的事情之前要
三思而後行。他們也可能會鼓勵孩子在學校裡努力一點，這樣將來人生的路才會有更多選

components.english: So, one major kind of advice parents give is practical guidance, because they want their children to avoid trouble and stand on their own two feet in the future.
components.chinese: 所以，父母給孩子其中一種很重要的建議，就是實際性的引導，因為他們希望孩子將來能避開麻煩，也能真正獨立起來。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one major kind of advice parents give is practical guidance,
because they want their children to avoid trouble and stand on their own two
feet in the future.
[所以，父母給孩子其中一種很重要的建議，就是實際性的引導，因為他們希望孩子將來能
避開麻煩，也能真正獨立起來。]

components.english: On top of that, parents also give a great deal of advice about values, character, and the way children treat other people.
components.chinese: 除此之外，父母也會給很多和價值觀、性格，以及孩子如何對待別人有關的建議。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: On top of that, parents also give a great deal of advice about values,
character, and the way children treat other people.
[除此之外，父母也會給很多和價值觀、性格，以及孩子如何對待別人有關的建議。]

components.english: To be honest, many parents are not only trying to raise successful children; they are trying to raise good human beings. That is why they often tell their children to be honest, respectful, kind, and responsible. These may sound like simple messages, but they shape the kind of person a child becomes. In other words, parents do not just want to fill their children’s heads; they also want to build their backbone.
components.chinese: 老實說，很多父母不只是想培養出一個成功的孩子；他們其實更想培養出一個好的人。所以，他們常常會教孩子要誠實、尊重別人、善良，以及有責任感。這些話聽起來也許很簡單，但它們其實會塑造一個孩子最後會成為怎樣的人。換句話說，父母不只是想把知識塞進孩子腦袋裡；他們同時也想建立孩子的骨氣和品格。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: To be honest, many parents are not only trying to raise
successful children; they are trying to raise good human beings. That is why
they often tell their children to be honest, respectful, kind, and responsible.
These may sound like simple messages, but they shape the kind of person a child
becomes. In other words, parents do not just want to fill their children’s heads;
they also want to build their backbone.
[老實說，很多父母不只是想培養出一個成功的孩子；他們其實更想培養出一個好的人。所
以，他們常常會教孩子要誠實、尊重別人、善良，以及有責任感。這些話聽起來也許很簡單，
但它們其實會塑造一個孩子最後會成為怎樣的人。換句話說，父母不只是想把知識塞進孩
子腦袋裡；他們同時也想建立孩子的骨氣和品格。]

components.english: For instance, a parent may tell a child not to lie even when it is easier, to say sorry when they are wrong, or to help others when they can. That kind of advice may not seem dramatic, but over time it sinks in and becomes part of the child’s inner compass.
components.chinese: 例如，父母可能會告訴孩子，即使說謊比較容易，也不要說謊；做錯了就要道歉；有能力的時候就應該幫助別人。這類建議看起來也許不算轟轟烈烈，但久而久之，它會慢慢沉澱下來，變成孩子心裡的指南針。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, a parent may tell a child not to lie even when it is
