easier, to say sorry when they are wrong, or to help others when they can. That
kind of advice may not seem dramatic, but over time it sinks in and becomes
part of the child’s inner compass.
[例如，父母可能會告訴孩子，即使說謊比較容易，也不要說謊；做錯了就要道歉；有能力的
時候就應該幫助別人。這類建議看起來也許不算轟轟烈烈，但久而久之，它會慢慢沉澱下來
，變成孩子心裡的指南針。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say parents usually advise their children on both life skills and life values. They want to help them do well, of course, but even more than that, they want to help them grow into capable, decent, and grounded adults.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說父母通常會同時給孩子生活技能和人生價值兩方面的建議。他們當然希望孩子過得好，但比起這一點更重要的，往往是希望孩子最後能成長為有能力、有分寸，而且腳踏實地的大人。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say parents usually advise their children on both
life skills and life values. They want to help them do well, of course, but even
more than that, they want to help them grow into capable, decent, and
grounded adults.
[總結來說，我會說父母通常會同時給孩子生活技能和人生價值兩方面的建議。他們當然希
望孩子過得好，但比起這一點更重要的，往往是希望孩子最後能成長為有能力、有分寸，而
且腳踏實地的大人。]

question.english: What kind of advice do friends give each other?
question.chinese: 朋友之間通常會給彼此甚麼樣的建議？

source_lines.text: Topic: What kind of advice do friends give each other?
朋友之間通常會給彼此甚麼樣的建議？

components.english: I think friends usually give each other advice about everyday life, especially things like study, relationships, stress, work, and personal decisions.
components.chinese: 我認為，朋友之間通常會就日常生活給彼此意見，特別是學業、感情、壓力、工作，以及一些個人決定這些方面。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think friends usually give each other advice about everyday life,
especially things like study, relationships, stress, work, and personal decisions.
[我認為，朋友之間通常會就日常生活給彼此意見，特別是學業、感情、壓力、工作，以及一
些個人決定這些方面。]

components.english: The main reason is that friends are often close to each other’s daily lives, so they understand the situation from the inside rather than from a distance. Because of that, their advice is usually practical, immediate, and relatable. It is often less formal than advice from parents or teachers, but sometimes that is exactly why it lands more naturally. A friend may not always give the wisest answer in the world, but they often know where the shoe pinches.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，朋友通常很貼近彼此的日常生活，所以他們對事情的理解很多時是從內部出發，而不是隔著一段距離去看。正因如此，他們給的建議通常都比較實際、即時，也較容易令人有共鳴。這些建議很多時沒有父母或老師的意見那麼正式，但有時也正正因為這樣，它反而更容易自然地說進人心裡。朋友未必每次都能給出世界上最有智慧的答案，但他們往往最知道問題真正卡在哪裡。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that friends are often close to each other’s
daily lives, so they understand the situation from the inside rather than from a
distance. Because of that, their advice is usually practical, immediate, and
relatable. It is often less formal than advice from parents or teachers, but
sometimes that is exactly why it lands more naturally. A friend may not always
give the wisest answer in the world, but they often know where the shoe
pinches.
[主要原因是，朋友通常很貼近彼此的日常生活，所以他們對事情的理解很多時是從內部出
發，而不是隔著一段距離去看。正因如此，他們給的建議通常都比較實際、即時，也較容易
令人有共鳴。這些建議很多時沒有父母或老師的意見那麼正式，但有時也正正因為這樣，它
反而更容易自然地說進人心裡。朋友未必每次都能給出世界上最有智慧的答案，但他們往
往最知道問題真正卡在哪裡。]

components.english: For example, if a student is feeling overwhelmed before exams, a friend might suggest making a simple revision plan, taking a short break, or stopping the panic before it snowballs. In the same way, if someone has had an argument with their partner, a friend may tell them to calm down first and talk things through instead of firing off another angry message.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個學生在考試前壓力很大，朋友可能會建議他先做一個簡單的溫習計劃、休息一下，或者先別讓焦慮越滾越大。同樣地，如果一個人和伴侶吵架了，朋友也可能會叫他先冷靜下來，再慢慢談清楚，而不是立刻再發一大串帶怒氣的訊息。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if a student is feeling overwhelmed before exams, a
friend might suggest making a simple revision plan, taking a short break, or
stopping the panic before it snowballs. In the same way, if someone has had an
argument with their partner, a friend may tell them to calm down first and talk
things through instead of firing off another angry message.
[例如，如果一個學生在考試前壓力很大，朋友可能會建議他先做一個簡單的溫習計劃、休
息一下，或者先別讓焦慮越滾越大。同樣地，如果一個人和伴侶吵架了，朋友也可能會叫他
先冷靜下來，再慢慢談清楚，而不是立刻再發一大串帶怒氣的訊息。]

components.english: So, in many cases, friends give advice that is down-to-earth and closely tied to the small but real problems people face every day.
components.chinese: 所以，在很多情況下，朋友給的建議都是比較貼地的，而且往往和人每天面對的那些細小但真實的問題很有關係。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, in many cases, friends give advice that is down-to-earth and
closely tied to the small but real problems people face every day.
[所以，在很多情況下，朋友給的建議都是比較貼地的，而且往往和人每天面對的那些細小
但真實的問題很有關係。]

components.english: That said, I think good friends do not only give practical advice; they also give emotional support and, sometimes, a much-needed reality check.
components.chinese: 不過，我也認為，好的朋友不只是提供實際建議；他們同時也會給予情感支持，而且有時還會給你一個很需要的當頭棒喝。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I think good friends do not only give practical advice; they
also give emotional support and, sometimes, a much-needed reality check.
[不過，我也認為，好的朋友不只是提供實際建議；他們同時也會給予情感支持，而且有時還
會給你一個很需要的當頭棒喝。]

components.english: To be honest, friendship is not just about making each other feel better. Sometimes it is about telling the truth gently but clearly. A real friend may encourage you when you are too hard on yourself, but they may also tell you when you are being unfair, reckless, or simply going round in circles. In that sense, a good friend is not just a shoulder to cry on; they are also a mirror that helps you see yourself more clearly.
components.chinese: 老實說，友情不只是讓彼此感覺好一點而已。有時候，它也關乎把真話用溫和但清楚的方式說出來。真正的朋友會在你對自己太苛刻時鼓勵你，但同時也可能在你不公平、太衝動，或者一直在原地打轉時提醒你。從這個角度來看，一位好朋友不只是讓你靠著哭的肩膀；他同時也是一面鏡子，幫助你更清楚地看見自己。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: To be honest, friendship is not just about making each other feel
better. Sometimes it is about telling the truth gently but clearly. A real friend
may encourage you when you are too hard on yourself, but they may also tell
you when you are being unfair, reckless, or simply going round in circles. In that
sense, a good friend is not just a shoulder to cry on; they are also a mirror that
helps you see yourself more clearly.
[老實說，友情不只是讓彼此感覺好一點而已。有時候，它也關乎把真話用溫和但清楚的方
式說出來。真正的朋友會在你對自己太苛刻時鼓勵你，但同時也可能在你不公平、太衝動，
或者一直在原地打轉時提醒你。從這個角度來看，一位好朋友不只是讓你靠著哭的肩膀；他
同時也是一面鏡子，幫助你更清楚地看見自己。]

components.english: For instance, if someone wants to give up on a goal too quickly, a friend may say, “Take a breath and sleep on it — don’t throw in the towel just because today was rough.” On the other hand, if that same person is staying in a toxic situation for too long, a good friend may tell them, with care, that enough is enough.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人太快就想放棄某個目標，朋友可能會對他說：「先深呼吸，睡一晚再想，不要只因為今天不順就立刻放棄。」相反，如果那個人已經在一個有害的處境裡拖得太久，一位真正好的朋友也可能會很有分寸地提醒他：是時候停下來了。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if someone wants to give up on a goal too quickly, a
friend may say, “Take a breath and sleep on it — don’t throw in the towel just
because today was rough.” On the other hand, if that same person is staying in a
toxic situation for too long, a good friend may tell them, with care, that enough
is enough.
[例如，如果一個人太快就想放棄某個目標，朋友可能會對他說：「先深呼吸，睡一晚再想，不
要只因為今天不順就立刻放棄。」相反，如果那個人已經在一個有害的處境裡拖得太久，一
位真正好的朋友也可能會很有分寸地提醒他：是時候停下來了。]

components.english: Overall, I would say friends usually give each other a mixture of practical advice, emotional reassurance, and honest perspective. The best advice from a friend is often not the most polished, but the most sincere — the kind that helps you think straighter, feel steadier, and move forward with a clearer head.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說朋友之間通常給彼此的，是實際建議、情感安慰，以及誠實角度的混合。來自朋友最好的建議，很多時未必是最完美包裝的，而是最真誠的那一種——那種能幫助你想得更清楚、心更穩，也更有方向地向前走的建議。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I would say friends usually give each other a mixture
of practical advice, emotional reassurance, and honest perspective. The best
advice from a friend is often not the most polished, but the most sincere — the
kind that helps you think straighter, feel steadier, and move forward with a
clearer head.
[總結來說，我會說朋友之間通常給彼此的，是實際建議、情感安慰，以及誠實角度的混合。
來自朋友最好的建議，很多時未必是最完美包裝的，而是最真誠的那一種——那種能幫助
你想得更清楚、心更穩，也更有方向地向前走的建議。]

question.english: What can we learn from our mistakes?
question.chinese: 我們能從錯誤中學到甚麼？

source_lines.text: Topic: What can we learn from our mistakes?
我們能從錯誤中學到甚麼？

components.english: I think we can learn a great deal from our mistakes, especially about what does not work, what we need to improve, and how to make better decisions next time.
components.chinese: 我認為，我們其實可以從錯誤中學到很多，尤其是明白甚麼方法行不通、自己有哪些地方需要改進，以及下次怎樣作出更好的決定。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think we can learn a great deal from our mistakes, especially about
what does not work, what we need to improve, and how to make better
decisions next time.
[我認為，我們其實可以從錯誤中學到很多，尤其是明白甚麼方法行不通、自己有哪些地方
需要改進，以及下次怎樣作出更好的決定。]

components.english: Very often, success feels smooth, so people enjoy it without examining it too closely. Mistakes, by contrast, force us to stop and pay attention. They show us our weak spots, our blind spots, and sometimes the gap between what we thought we were doing and what we were actually doing. In that sense, mistakes can be hard teachers, but they are often honest ones.
components.chinese: 很多時候，成功來得比較順，人們往往只是享受成果，未必會仔細拆解當中的原因。相反，錯誤會逼使我們停下來，好好留意問題所在。它們會讓我們看見自己的弱點、盲點，有時甚至會揭示出「我們以為自己在做甚麼」和「我們實際上在做甚麼」之間的距離。從這個角度來說，錯誤也許是很嚴厲的老師，但往往也是很誠實的老師。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: Very often, success feels smooth, so people enjoy it without
examining it too closely. Mistakes, by contrast, force us to stop and pay
attention. They show us our weak spots, our blind spots, and sometimes the gap
between what we thought we were doing and what we were actually doing. In
that sense, mistakes can be hard teachers, but they are often honest ones.
[很多時候，成功來得比較順，人們往往只是享受成果，未必會仔細拆解當中的原因。相反，
錯誤會逼使我們停下來，好好留意問題所在。它們會讓我們看見自己的弱點、盲點，有時甚
至會揭示出「我們以為自己在做甚麼」和「我們實際上在做甚麼」之間的距離。從這個角度來
說，錯誤也許是很嚴厲的老師，但往往也是很誠實的老師。]

components.english: For example, if a student performs badly in an exam because they left revision until the last minute, the mistake can teach them something very practical: good intentions are not enough, and time management really matters. The poor result may sting at first, but it can still become a turning point.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一位學生因為把溫習拖到最後一刻而考得不好，這個錯誤就能教會他一個很實際的道理：單有好意圖並不足夠，時間管理真的很重要。那個不理想的成績一開始也許會令人難受，但它仍然可以成為一個轉捩點。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if a student performs badly in an exam because they
left revision until the last minute, the mistake can teach them something very
practical: good intentions are not enough, and time management really matters.
The poor result may sting at first, but it can still become a turning point.
[例如，如果一位學生因為把溫習拖到最後一刻而考得不好，這個錯誤就能教會他一個很實
際的道理：單有好意圖並不足夠，時間管理真的很重要。那個不理想的成績一開始也許會令
人難受，但它仍然可以成為一個轉捩點。]

components.english: So, one major thing we can learn from our mistakes is practical wisdom, because they help us adjust our habits, sharpen our judgment, and avoid falling into the same trap again.
components.chinese: 所以，我們能從錯誤中學到的一大重點，就是實際的智慧，因為錯誤能幫助我們調整習慣、磨練判斷力，並避免再次掉進同一個陷阱。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one major thing we can learn from our mistakes is practical
wisdom, because they help us adjust our habits, sharpen our judgment, and
avoid falling into the same trap again.
[所以，我們能從錯誤中學到的一大重點，就是實際的智慧，因為錯誤能幫助我們調整習
慣、磨練判斷力，並避免再次掉進同一個陷阱。]

components.english: On top of that, I think mistakes can also teach us deeper personal lessons, such as humility, patience, and empathy.
components.chinese: 除此之外，我也認為錯誤能教會我們一些更深層的人生成長，例如謙遜、耐性，以及同理心。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: On top of that, I think mistakes can also teach us deeper personal
lessons, such as humility, patience, and empathy.
[除此之外，我也認為錯誤能教會我們一些更深層的人生成長，例如謙遜、耐性，以及同理

components.english: When people make mistakes, they are reminded that they are human, not perfect. That can make them less arrogant and more understanding towards others. It can also build resilience, because people learn that getting something wrong is not the end of the world; it is often part of the road. In other words, mistakes do not just correct our actions; they can soften our ego and strengthen our character.
components.chinese: 當人犯錯時，他們會被提醒：自己終究是人，而不是完美無缺的。這樣一來，人就可能變得沒那麼自以為是，也更能理解別人的失誤。錯誤同時也能培養韌性，因為人會慢慢明白：做錯並不代表世界末日，它很多時只是成長路上的一部分。換句話說，錯誤不只是修正我們的行為，也可能磨柔我們的自我，並加強我們的品格。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: When people make mistakes, they are reminded that they are
human, not perfect. That can make them less arrogant and more understanding
towards others. It can also build resilience, because people learn that getting
something wrong is not the end of the world; it is often part of the road. In
other words, mistakes do not just correct our actions; they can soften our ego
and strengthen our character.
[當人犯錯時，他們會被提醒：自己終究是人，而不是完美無缺的。這樣一來，人就可能變得
沒那麼自以為是，也更能理解別人的失誤。錯誤同時也能培養韌性，因為人會慢慢明白：做
錯並不代表世界末日，它很多時只是成長路上的一部分。換句話說，錯誤不只是修正我們的
行為，也可能磨柔我們的自我，並加強我們的品格。]

components.english: For instance, if someone says something careless and ends up hurting a friend’s feelings, they may later learn to think before they speak and to handle other people’s emotions more gently. That kind of lesson goes beyond technique; it becomes part of who they are.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人因為說話不夠小心而傷害了朋友的感受，他之後就可能學會先想清楚才開口，也會更懂得溫柔地處理別人的情緒。這種學到的東西已經不只是技巧，而會慢慢成為他這個人的一部分。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, if someone says something careless and ends up
hurting a friend’s feelings, they may later learn to think before they speak and
to handle other people’s emotions more gently. That kind of lesson goes beyond
technique; it becomes part of who they are.
[例如，如果一個人因為說話不夠小心而傷害了朋友的感受，他之後就可能學會先想清楚才
開口，也會更懂得溫柔地處理別人的情緒。這種學到的東西已經不只是技巧，而會慢慢成為
他這個人的一部分。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say we can learn both practical lessons and personal lessons from our mistakes. In the end, a mistake is only wasted if we learn nothing from it; if we reflect on it honestly, it can become a stepping stone rather than a stumbling block.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說我們既能從錯誤中學到實際層面的教訓，也能學到個人成長層面的道理。說到底，只有在我們甚麼都不學的時候，錯誤才算真正被浪費；如果我們願意誠實地反思，它就可以成為踏腳石，而不是絆腳石。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say we can learn both practical lessons and personal
lessons from our mistakes. In the end, a mistake is only wasted if we learn
nothing from it; if we reflect on it honestly, it can become a stepping stone
rather than a stumbling block.
[總結來說，我會說我們既能從錯誤中學到實際層面的教訓，也能學到個人成長層面的道
理。說到底，只有在我們甚麼都不學的時候，錯誤才算真正被浪費；如果我們願意誠實地反
思，它就可以成為踏腳石，而不是絆腳石。]

question.english: Do children make mistakes easily?
question.chinese: 小孩子是否很容易犯錯？

source_lines.text: Topic: Do children make mistakes easily?
小孩子是否很容易犯錯？

components.english: Yes, I think children do make mistakes quite easily, and that is perfectly natural.
components.chinese: 是的，我認為小孩子的確很容易犯錯，而那其實是很自然的事。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, I think children do make mistakes quite easily, and that is perfectly
natural.
[是的，我認為小孩子的確很容易犯錯，而那其實是很自然的事。]

components.english: The main reason is that children are still learning how the world works. They do not yet have the experience, judgment, or self-control that adults usually develop over time. So they may say the wrong thing, trust the wrong person, forget instructions, or act without thinking things through. In that sense, mistakes are almost part and parcel of childhood.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，孩子仍然在學習這個世界是怎樣運作的。他們還沒有成年人通常會慢慢建立起來的經驗、判斷力，以及自制能力。所以，他們可能會說錯話、信錯人、忘記指示，或者在沒有想清楚的情況下就先行動。從這個角度來看，犯錯幾乎本來就是童年的一部分。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that children are still learning how the
world works. They do not yet have the experience, judgment, or self-control
that adults usually develop over time. So they may say the wrong thing, trust
the wrong person, forget instructions, or act without thinking things through.
In that sense, mistakes are almost part and parcel of childhood.
[主要原因是，孩子仍然在學習這個世界是怎樣運作的。他們還沒有成年人通常會慢慢建立
起來的經驗、判斷力，以及自制能力。所以，他們可能會說錯話、信錯人、忘記指示，或者在
沒有想清楚的情況下就先行動。從這個角度來看，犯錯幾乎本來就是童年的一部分。]

components.english: For example, a child may spill something, lose their homework, or argue with a friend over something small. To adults, those mistakes may look obvious, but to the child, they are often just part of figuring life out one step at a time.
components.chinese: 例如，一個孩子可能會打翻東西、弄丟功課，或者因為一些小事和朋友吵架。對成年人來說，這些錯誤也許看起來很明顯，但對孩子而言，那很多時都只是他們一步一步摸索人生的一部分。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, a child may spill something, lose their homework, or
argue with a friend over something small. To adults, those mistakes may look
obvious, but to the child, they are often just part of figuring life out one step at
a time.
[例如，一個孩子可能會打翻東西、弄丟功課，或者因為一些小事和朋友吵架。對成年人來
說，這些錯誤也許看起來很明顯，但對孩子而言，那很多時都只是他們一步一步摸索人生的
一部分。]

components.english: So yes, children do make mistakes easily, mainly because they are still growing, learning, and testing the world around them.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，孩子的確很容易犯錯，主要是因為他們仍然在成長、學習，並且不斷試探自己周圍的世界。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, children do make mistakes easily, mainly because they are
still growing, learning, and testing the world around them.
[所以，是的，孩子的確很容易犯錯，主要是因為他們仍然在成長、學習，並且不斷試探自己
周圍的世界。]

components.english: That said, I do not think this is a bad thing, because mistakes are often how children learn best.
components.chinese: 不過，我並不認為這是壞事，因為錯誤很多時候正正是孩子學得最好的方式。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think this is a bad thing, because mistakes are often
how children learn best.
[不過，我並不認為這是壞事，因為錯誤很多時候正正是孩子學得最好的方式。]

components.english: If children were never allowed to make mistakes, they might become too fearful, too dependent, or too afraid to try new things. A mistake can teach responsibility, caution, and resilience far more effectively than a lecture sometimes can. In other words, children do not just grow by being corrected; they also grow by stumbling, reflecting, and trying again.
components.chinese: 如果孩子從來都不被容許犯錯，他們反而可能會變得太害怕、太依賴別人，或者不敢再嘗試新事物。有時候，一次錯誤能教會孩子責任感、謹慎，以及韌性，而效果甚至比一場說教更大。換句話說，孩子並不只是透過被糾正而成長；他們很多時候也是透過跌一跌、想一想，再重新來過而長大的。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: If children were never allowed to make mistakes, they might
become too fearful, too dependent, or too afraid to try new things. A mistake
can teach responsibility, caution, and resilience far more effectively than a
lecture sometimes can. In other words, children do not just grow by being
corrected; they also grow by stumbling, reflecting, and trying again.
[如果孩子從來都不被容許犯錯，他們反而可能會變得太害怕、太依賴別人，或者不敢再嘗
試新事物。有時候，一次錯誤能教會孩子責任感、謹慎，以及韌性，而效果甚至比一場說教
更大。換句話說，孩子並不只是透過被糾正而成長；他們很多時候也是透過跌一跌、想一想
，再重新來過而長大的。]

components.english: For instance, if a child spends all their pocket money too quickly and later regrets it, that experience may teach them more about money than simply being told to save. The lesson bites a little, but it sticks.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個孩子太快把零用錢花光，之後又感到後悔，那次經驗很多時候會比單單被告知要儲錢，更能教會他金錢觀。那個教訓也許會有一點刺痛，但也正因如此，它更容易留在心裡。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if a child spends all their pocket money too quickly
and later regrets it, that experience may teach them more about money than
simply being told to save. The lesson bites a little, but it sticks.
[例如，如果一個孩子太快把零用錢花光，之後又感到後悔，那次經驗很多時候會比單單被
告知要儲錢，更能教會他金錢觀。那個教訓也許會有一點刺痛，但也正因如此，它更容易留
在心裡。]

components.english: Overall, I would say children do make mistakes easily, but that is not something to fear too much. As long as they are guided with patience and care, mistakes can become stepping stones rather than stumbling blocks.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說孩子的確很容易犯錯，但那並不是一件需要過分害怕的事。只要他們在耐心和關心之下被好好引導，錯誤很多時候就能成為踏腳石，而不是絆腳石。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I would say children do make mistakes easily, but that
is not something to fear too much. As long as they are guided with patience and
care, mistakes can become stepping stones rather than stumbling blocks.
[總結來說，我會說孩子的確很容易犯錯，但那並不是一件需要過分害怕的事。只要他們在
耐心和關心之下被好好引導，錯誤很多時候就能成為踏腳石，而不是絆腳石。]

question.english: What do children learn from teachers and parents?
question.chinese: 孩子會從老師和父母身上學到甚麼？

source_lines.text: Topic: What do children learn from teachers and parents?
[孩子會從老師和父母身上學到甚麼？]

components.english: From teachers, children mainly learn knowledge, discipline, and how to learn in a structured way.
components.chinese: 從老師身上，孩子主要學到的是知識、紀律，以及如何在有系統的環境中學習。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: From teachers, children mainly learn knowledge, discipline, and how to
learn in a structured way.
[從老師身上，孩子主要學到的是知識、紀律，以及如何在有系統的環境中學習。]

components.english: Teachers are usually the people who open the academic door. They help children build literacy, numeracy, curiosity, and the habit of asking better questions. On top of that, school is often where children learn to follow rules, work with classmates, and cope with deadlines or pressure. In that sense, a good teacher does not just fill a child’s head; they also sharpen the tools inside it.
components.chinese: 老師通常是替孩子打開學習之門的人。他們幫助孩子建立語文能力、數理能力、好奇心，以及提出更好問題的習慣。除此之外，學校往往也是孩子學會守規矩、與同學合作，以及面對截止日期和壓力的地方。從這個角度來說，一位好老師不只是把知識放進孩子腦袋裡；他們同時也在磨利孩子思考的工具。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: Teachers are usually the people who open the academic door.
They help children build literacy, numeracy, curiosity, and the habit of asking
better questions. On top of that, school is often where children learn to follow
rules, work with classmates, and cope with deadlines or pressure. In that sense, a
good teacher does not just fill a child’s head; they also sharpen the tools inside it.
[老師通常是替孩子打開學習之門的人。他們幫助孩子建立語文能力、數理能力、好奇心，
以及提出更好問題的習慣。除此之外，學校往往也是孩子學會守規矩、與同學合作，以及面
對截止日期和壓力的地方。從這個角度來說，一位好老師不只是把知識放進孩子腦袋裡；他
們同時也在磨利孩子思考的工具。]

components.english: For example, a teacher may show a child how to solve a maths problem, write a clear essay, or speak up in class with confidence. Sometimes even one encouraging comment from a teacher can light a fire under a student and make them believe they are capable of more than they thought.
components.chinese: 例如，老師可以教孩子怎樣解數學題、怎樣寫一篇清楚的文章，或者怎樣有自信地在課堂上發言。有時候，老師一句鼓勵的話，就已經足以點燃一個學生的動力，讓他相信自己其實比想像中更有能力。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, a teacher may show a child how to solve a maths
problem, write a clear essay, or speak up in class with confidence. Sometimes
even one encouraging comment from a teacher can light a fire under a student
and make them believe they are capable of more than they thought.
[例如，老師可以教孩子怎樣解數學題、怎樣寫一篇清楚的文章，或者怎樣有自信地在課堂
上發言。有時候，老師一句鼓勵的話，就已經足以點燃一個學生的動力，讓他相信自己其實
比想像中更有能力。]

components.english: So, from teachers, children often learn not only what to study, but also how to think, focus, and keep improving.
components.chinese: 所以，孩子從老師身上學到的，不只是要學甚麼，也包括如何思考、如何專注，以及如何持續進步。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, from teachers, children often learn not only what to study,
but also how to think, focus, and keep improving.
[所以，孩子從老師身上學到的，不只是要學甚麼，也包括如何思考、如何專注，以及如何持
續進步。]

components.english: From parents, children usually learn values, habits, and ways of dealing with life itself.
components.chinese: 從父母身上，孩子通常學到的是價值觀、生活習慣，以及面對人生的方法。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: From parents, children usually learn values, habits, and ways of
dealing with life itself.
[從父母身上，孩子通常學到的是價值觀、生活習慣，以及面對人生的方法。]

components.english: Parents are a child’s first teachers in the deepest sense. At home, children learn how to speak to others, how to handle disappointment, how to show kindness, and how to take responsibility. A lot of that learning is caught rather than taught. Children listen with their ears, but they learn even more with their eyes, because they are constantly watching how adults behave when life is easy and when life gets messy.
components.chinese: 父母其實是孩子最早、也最深層意義上的老師。在家裡，孩子會學到如何和別人說話、如何面對失望、如何表達善意，以及如何承擔責任。而且，這些學習很多時候不是靠直接說教，而是靠耳濡目染。孩子固然會用耳朵去聽，但他們其實更常是用眼睛去學，因為他們一直在看大人如何在順利時表現，也如何在混亂時應對。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: Parents are a child’s first teachers in the deepest sense. At
home, children learn how to speak to others, how to handle disappointment,
how to show kindness, and how to take responsibility. A lot of that learning is
caught rather than taught. Children listen with their ears, but they learn even
more with their eyes, because they are constantly watching how adults behave
when life is easy and when life gets messy.
[父母其實是孩子最早、也最深層意義上的老師。在家裡，孩子會學到如何和別人說話、如
何面對失望、如何表達善意，以及如何承擔責任。而且，這些學習很多時候不是靠直接說教
，而是靠耳濡目染。孩子固然會用耳朵去聽，但他們其實更常是用眼睛去學，因為他們一直
在看大人如何在順利時表現，也如何在混亂時應對。]

components.english: For instance, if parents say thank you, apologise when they are wrong, and treat other people with respect, children are likely to absorb those values naturally. In the same way, if parents stay calm under pressure or keep their word, children learn that character is shown in actions, not just in speeches.
components.chinese: 例如，如果父母會說謝謝、做錯時願意道歉，並且以尊重的方式對待別人，孩子通常也會很自然地吸收這些價值。同樣地，如果父母在壓力下仍然保持冷靜，或者說到做到，孩子也會明白：品格不是只靠說出來，而是靠行動顯示出來。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, if parents say thank you, apologise when they are
wrong, and treat other people with respect, children are likely to absorb those
values naturally. In the same way, if parents stay calm under pressure or keep
their word, children learn that character is shown in actions, not just in
speeches.
[例如，如果父母會說謝謝、做錯時願意道歉，並且以尊重的方式對待別人，孩子通常也會
很自然地吸收這些價值。同樣地，如果父母在壓力下仍然保持冷靜，或者說到做到，孩子也
會明白：品格不是只靠說出來，而是靠行動顯示出來。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say teachers shape the mind, while parents shape the person. When both sides pull in the same direction, children are far more likely to grow into capable, thoughtful, and decent adults.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說老師塑造的是頭腦，而父母塑造的是整個人。當這兩方面朝同一個方向用力時，孩子就更有可能成長為有能力、有思考，而且品格端正的大人。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say teachers shape the mind, while parents shape
the person. When both sides pull in the same direction, children are far more
likely to grow into capable, thoughtful, and decent adults.
[總結來說，我會說老師塑造的是頭腦，而父母塑造的是整個人。當這兩方面朝同一個方向
用力時，孩子就更有可能成長為有能力、有思考，而且品格端正的大人。]

question.english: What should parents do if their children make mistakes?
question.chinese: 如果孩子犯錯，父母應該怎樣做？

source_lines.text: Topic: What should parents do if their children make mistakes?
如果孩子犯錯，父母應該怎樣做？

components.english: I think parents should respond with patience and guidance rather than immediate anger, because a mistake is often a chance to teach, not just a reason to punish.
components.chinese: 我認為，父母應該以耐心和引導去回應，而不是立刻發怒，因為犯錯很多時其實是一個教育機會，而不只是懲罰的理由。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think parents should respond with patience and guidance rather than
immediate anger, because a mistake is often a chance to teach, not just a reason
to punish.
[我認為，父母應該以耐心和引導去回應，而不是立刻發怒，因為犯錯很多時其實是一個教
育機會，而不只是懲罰的理由。]

components.english: Children are still learning how the world works, so mistakes are part and parcel of growing up. If parents react too harshly, the child may become fearful, defensive, or simply learn to hide the truth next time. By contrast, if parents stay calm and explain why something was wrong, the child is much more likely to reflect on it and actually learn the lesson. In that sense, a mistake should be treated more like a classroom than a courtroom.
components.chinese: 孩子本來就在學習怎樣理解世界，所以犯錯本來就是成長的一部分。如果父母反應太激烈，孩子可能會變得害怕、抗拒，或者下次乾脆選擇隱瞞。相反，如果父母能保持冷靜，並清楚解釋事情為甚麼不對，孩子就更有可能真正反思，並且學到教訓。從這個角度來看，錯誤更應該像一間課室，而不是一個法庭。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: Children are still learning how the world works, so mistakes are
part and parcel of growing up. If parents react too harshly, the child may
become fearful, defensive, or simply learn to hide the truth next time. By
contrast, if parents stay calm and explain why something was wrong, the child is
much more likely to reflect on it and actually learn the lesson. In that sense, a
mistake should be treated more like a classroom than a courtroom.
[孩子本來就在學習怎樣理解世界，所以犯錯本來就是成長的一部分。如果父母反應太激烈
，孩子可能會變得害怕、抗拒，或者下次乾脆選擇隱瞞。相反，如果父母能保持冷靜，並清楚
解釋事情為甚麼不對，孩子就更有可能真正反思，並且學到教訓。從這個角度來看，錯誤更
應該像一間課室，而不是一個法庭。]

components.english: For example, if a child lies about not doing their homework, shouting at them may only make them more nervous and secretive. However, if the parent talks it through, explains why honesty matters, and helps the child face the consequence, the lesson is likely to sink in much more deeply.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個孩子說謊，假裝自己已經做完功課，父母若只是大聲責罵，很多時只會令他更緊張，也更想把事情藏起來。不過，如果父母願意把事情談清楚、解釋誠實的重要，並幫助孩子面對後果，那個教訓往往會更深刻地留在心裡。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if a child lies about not doing their homework,
shouting at them may only make them more nervous and secretive. However, if
the parent talks it through, explains why honesty matters, and helps the child
face the consequence, the lesson is likely to sink in much more deeply.
[例如，如果一個孩子說謊，假裝自己已經做完功課，父母若只是大聲責罵，很多時只會令
他更緊張，也更想把事情藏起來。不過，如果父母願意把事情談清楚、解釋誠實的重要，並
幫助孩子面對後果，那個教訓往往會更深刻地留在心裡。]

components.english: So, in my view, parents should try to turn mistakes into learning moments, because the goal is not just to stop the behaviour, but to help the child grow beyond it.
components.chinese: 所以，在我看來，父母應該盡量把錯誤變成學習時刻，因為真正的目的不只是阻止某個行為，而是幫助孩子從中成長。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, in my view, parents should try to turn mistakes into learning
moments, because the goal is not just to stop the behaviour, but to help the child
grow beyond it.
[所以，在我看來，父母應該盡量把錯誤變成學習時刻，因為真正的目的不只是阻止某個行
為，而是幫助孩子從中成長。]

components.english: That said, being understanding does not mean letting children off the hook, because mistakes still need consequences and responsibility.
components.chinese: 不過，願意理解孩子，並不代表要輕輕放過，因為錯誤仍然需要有後果，也需要承擔責任。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, being understanding does not mean letting children off the
hook, because mistakes still need consequences and responsibility.
[不過，願意理解孩子，並不代表要輕輕放過，因為錯誤仍然需要有後果，也需要承擔責

components.english: If parents are kind but never firm, the lesson can lose its teeth. Children need to see that actions have consequences, otherwise the message stays soft around the edges and never really lands. The key, I think, is balance: correct the behaviour without crushing the child, and set boundaries without turning the home into a battlefield. Warmth matters, but so does backbone.
components.chinese: 如果父母只有溫和，卻從不堅定，那個教訓就可能變得沒有力量。孩子需要明白，行為是有後果的，否則那個訊息就會一直模模糊糊，最後根本進不了心。對我來說，關鍵就在於平衡：要糾正行為，但不要傷害孩子本身；要立下界線，但也不要把家變成戰場。溫度很重要，但原則也同樣重要。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: If parents are kind but never firm, the lesson can lose its teeth.
Children need to see that actions have consequences, otherwise the message stays
soft around the edges and never really lands. The key, I think, is balance: correct
the behaviour without crushing the child, and set boundaries without turning
the home into a battlefield. Warmth matters, but so does backbone.
[如果父母只有溫和，卻從不堅定，那個教訓就可能變得沒有力量。孩子需要明白，行為是
有後果的，否則那個訊息就會一直模模糊糊，最後根本進不了心。對我來說，關鍵就在於平
衡：要糾正行為，但不要傷害孩子本身；要立下界線，但也不要把家變成戰場。溫度很重要，
但原則也同樣重要。]

components.english: For instance, if a child breaks something through carelessness, the parent might ask them to apologise, help clean it up, or use some pocket money to replace it. That way, the child learns accountability, but still feels supported rather than shamed.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個孩子因為粗心而弄壞了東西，父母可以要求他道歉、一起收拾善後，或者用部分零用錢去補償。這樣一來，孩子就能學會承擔責任，但同時仍然感受到自己是被支持的，而不是被羞辱的。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, if a child breaks something through carelessness, the
parent might ask them to apologise, help clean it up, or use some pocket money
to replace it. That way, the child learns accountability, but still feels supported
rather than shamed.
[例如，如果一個孩子因為粗心而弄壞了東西，父母可以要求他道歉、一起收拾善後，或者
用部分零用錢去補償。這樣一來，孩子就能學會承擔責任，但同時仍然感受到自己是被支持
的，而不是被羞辱的。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say parents should meet children’s mistakes with both compassion and firmness. The best approach is neither constant blame nor blind indulgence, but teaching children, through their mistakes, how to become better people.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，父母應該用同理心和原則去面對孩子的錯誤。最好的做法不是一味責備，也不是一味縱容，而是在錯誤裡教會孩子怎樣成為更好的人。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say parents should meet children’s mistakes with
both compassion and firmness. The best approach is neither constant blame nor
blind indulgence, but teaching children, through their mistakes, how to become
better people.
[總結來說，我會說，父母應該用同理心和原則去面對孩子的錯誤。最好的做法不是一味責
備，也不是一味縱容，而是在錯誤裡教會孩子怎樣成為更好的人。]

question.english: How should a person be remorseful for their mistakes?
question.chinese: 一個人應該怎樣為自己的錯誤感到悔意，並表現出悔意？

source_lines.text: Topic: How should a person be remorseful for their mistakes?
一個人應該怎樣為自己的錯誤感到悔意，並表現出悔意？

components.english: I think a person should show remorse first by admitting the mistake honestly and taking responsibility for it.
components.chinese: 我認為，一個人若真的有悔意，首先應該誠實承認自己的錯誤，並且為它負起責任。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think a person should show remorse first by admitting the mistake
honestly and taking responsibility for it.
[我認為，一個人若真的有悔意，首先應該誠實承認自己的錯誤，並且為它負起責任。]

components.english: To me, real remorse is not just feeling bad inside. It begins with facing the truth instead of hiding behind excuses. If someone keeps blaming stress, bad luck, or other people, their regret can sound a bit hollow. Owning the mistake matters, because that is the moment when remorse stops being a feeling and starts becoming a moral response.
components.chinese: 對我來說，真正的悔意不只是心裡難受而已。它是從正面面對事實開始的，而不是躲在藉口後面。如果一個人不停把責任推給壓力、運氣不好，或者別人，那麼他的後悔聽起來就會有點空洞。願意承認錯誤很重要，因為那正是悔意從一種情緒，變成一種道德回應的起點。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: To me, real remorse is not just feeling bad inside. It begins with
facing the truth instead of hiding behind excuses. If someone keeps blaming
stress, bad luck, or other people, their regret can sound a bit hollow. Owning
the mistake matters, because that is the moment when remorse stops being a
feeling and starts becoming a moral response.
[對我來說，真正的悔意不只是心裡難受而已。它是從正面面對事實開始的，而不是躲在藉
口後面。如果一個人不停把責任推給壓力、運氣不好，或者別人，那麼他的後悔聽起來就會
有點空洞。願意承認錯誤很重要，因為那正是悔意從一種情緒，變成一種道德回應的起點。]

components.english: For example, if someone says something hurtful to a friend, the first step should be to admit it plainly, rather than saying, “Well, I was tired,” or “You made me angry.” A simple, honest sentence like “I was wrong, and I should not have said that” usually carries much more weight.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人對朋友說了很傷人的話，第一步應該是坦白承認，而不是說「我只是太累了」或者「是你先惹我生氣」。一句簡單而真誠的話，例如「是我錯了，我不應該那樣說」，往往會更有分量。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if someone says something hurtful to a friend, the first
step should be to admit it plainly, rather than saying, “Well, I was tired,” or
“You made me angry.” A simple, honest sentence like “I was wrong, and I should
not have said that” usually carries much more weight.
[例如，如果一個人對朋友說了很傷人的話，第一步應該是坦白承認，而不是說「我只是太
累了」或者「是你先惹我生氣」。一句簡單而真誠的話，例如「是我錯了，我不應該那樣說」，
往往會更有分量。]

components.english: So, in my view, remorse should begin with honesty, humility, and a willingness to stop dodging responsibility.
components.chinese: 所以，在我看來，悔意應該從誠實、謙卑，以及不再逃避責任開始。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, in my view, remorse should begin with honesty, humility, and
a willingness to stop dodging responsibility.
[所以，在我看來，悔意應該從誠實、謙卑，以及不再逃避責任開始。]

components.english: That said, I do not think remorse should end with guilt alone; it should also lead to apology, repair, and real change.
components.chinese: 不過，我也不認為悔意只停留在內疚就夠了；它還應該進一步帶來道歉、補救，以及真正的改變。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think remorse should end with guilt alone; it
should also lead to apology, repair, and real change.
[不過，我也不認為悔意只停留在內疚就夠了；它還應該進一步帶來道歉、補救，以及真正
的改變。]

components.english: Feeling sorry is important, of course, but actions are where remorse earns its stripes. If someone truly regrets what they did, they should try to make amends and avoid repeating the same mistake. Otherwise, the remorse stays at the level of emotion and never grows into character. In other words, a sincere apology opens the door, but changed behaviour is what walks through it.
components.chinese: 當然，感到抱歉是重要的，但真正能證明悔意的，還是行動。如果一個人真的後悔自己做過的事，他就應該嘗試補救，並且避免重蹈覆轍。否則，那份悔意就只停留在情緒層面，而沒有真正長進成品格。換句話說，真誠的道歉只是把門打開，而真正走進那道門的，是改變了的行為。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: Feeling sorry is important, of course, but actions are where
remorse earns its stripes. If someone truly regrets what they did, they should try
to make amends and avoid repeating the same mistake. Otherwise, the remorse
stays at the level of emotion and never grows into character. In other words, a
sincere apology opens the door, but changed behaviour is what walks through it.
[當然，感到抱歉是重要的，但真正能證明悔意的，還是行動。如果一個人真的後悔自己做
過的事，他就應該嘗試補救，並且避免重蹈覆轍。否則，那份悔意就只停留在情緒層面，而
沒有真正長進成品格。換句話說，真誠的道歉只是把門打開，而真正走進那道門的，是改變
了的行為。]

components.english: For instance, if a person makes a serious mistake at work, it is not enough just to say sorry once and move on. They should apologise properly, help fix the problem, and show through later actions that they have learnt the lesson. That kind of remorse feels far more genuine than dramatic regret with no follow-through.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人在工作上犯了嚴重錯誤，那麼只說一句對不起，然後若無其事地走開，其實並不足夠。他應該好好道歉、幫忙補救問題，並且在之後用行動證明自己真的學到了教訓。這種悔意會比那種很戲劇化、卻沒有後續行動的懊悔真實得多。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if a person makes a serious mistake at work, it is not
enough just to say sorry once and move on. They should apologise properly, help
fix the problem, and show through later actions that they have learnt the lesson.
That kind of remorse feels far more genuine than dramatic regret with no
follow-through.
[例如，如果一個人在工作上犯了嚴重錯誤，那麼只說一句對不起，然後若無其事地走開，
其實並不足夠。他應該好好道歉、幫忙補救問題，並且在之後用行動證明自己真的學到了
教訓。這種悔意會比那種很戲劇化、卻沒有後續行動的懊悔真實得多。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say a person should be remorseful by owning the mistake, apologising sincerely, trying to repair the damage, and changing afterwards. Real remorse is not just about feeling the sting of the mistake; it is about letting that sting teach you how to do better next time.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，一個人若要真正表現出悔意，就應該承認錯誤、真誠道歉、努力補救，並且在之後有所改變。真正的悔意，不只是感受到錯誤帶來的刺痛；更重要的是，讓那份刺痛教會你下次做得更好。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, I’d say a person should be remorseful by owning the
mistake, apologising sincerely, trying to repair the damage, and changing
afterwards. Real remorse is not just about feeling the sting of the mistake; it is
about letting that sting teach you how to do better next time.
[總結來說，我會說，一個人若要真正表現出悔意，就應該承認錯誤、真誠道歉、努力補救，
並且在之後有所改變。真正的悔意，不只是感受到錯誤帶來的刺痛；更重要的是，讓那份刺
痛教會你下次做得更好。]

question.english: How do children benefit from using dictionaries?
question.chinese: 孩子使用字典有甚麼好處？

source_lines.text: Topic: How do children benefit from using dictionaries?
孩子使用字典有甚麼好處？

components.english: I think children benefit from using dictionaries because dictionaries help them become more independent, more accurate, and more confident learners.
components.chinese: 我認為，孩子使用字典會受益，因為字典能幫助他們成為更獨立、更準確，也更有自信的學習者。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think children benefit from using dictionaries because dictionaries
help them become more independent, more accurate, and more confident
learners.
[我認為，孩子使用字典會受益，因為字典能幫助他們成為更獨立、更準確，也更有自信的
學習者。]

components.english: The main reason is that a dictionary teaches children how to find answers for themselves instead of relying on other people every time they get stuck. When they look up a word, they are not just learning one meaning; they are often learning the spelling, pronunciation, part of speech, and how the word is actually used. In that sense, a dictionary is a bit like a small language coach sitting quietly in their hands. It helps them stop guessing in the dark and start learning with more precision.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，字典能教孩子在遇到不懂的地方時，學會自己找答案，而不是每一次都依賴別人。當他們查一個字時，他們學到的往往不只是一個意思；很多時還包括拼寫、發音、詞性，以及那個字實際上怎樣使用。從這個角度來看，字典有點像一位安靜地待在孩子手中的小語言教練。它能幫助他們不再在黑暗中亂猜，而是更準確地學習。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that a dictionary teaches children how to
find answers for themselves instead of relying on other people every time they
get stuck. When they look up a word, they are not just learning one meaning;
they are often learning the spelling, pronunciation, part of speech, and how the
word is actually used. In that sense, a dictionary is a bit like a small language
coach sitting quietly in their hands. It helps them stop guessing in the dark and
start learning with more precision.
[主要原因是，字典能教孩子在遇到不懂的地方時，學會自己找答案，而不是每一次都依賴
別人。當他們查一個字時，他們學到的往往不只是一個意思；很多時還包括拼寫、發音、詞
性，以及那個字實際上怎樣使用。從這個角度來看，字典有點像一位安靜地待在孩子手中的
小語言教練。它能幫助他們不再在黑暗中亂猜，而是更準確地學習。]

components.english: For example, if a child is reading a story and comes across an unfamiliar word, they can look it up and understand not only what it means, but also how to use it in another sentence. That makes the learning stick much better than simply asking someone for a quick translation.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個孩子在閱讀故事時遇到一個不熟悉的詞，他可以自己查字典，明白它是甚麼意思，甚至知道它可以怎樣放進另一個句子裡。這種學習方式通常比單純向別人問一個快速翻譯，更容易真正記得住。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if a child is reading a story and comes across an
unfamiliar word, they can look it up and understand not only what it means,
but also how to use it in another sentence. That makes the learning stick much
better than simply asking someone for a quick translation.
[例如，如果一個孩子在閱讀故事時遇到一個不熟悉的詞，他可以自己查字典，明白它是甚
麼意思，甚至知道它可以怎樣放進另一個句子裡。這種學習方式通常比單純向別人問一個
快速翻譯，更容易真正記得住。]

components.english: So, one clear benefit is that dictionaries help children build vocabulary and learn how to stand on their own feet academically.
components.chinese: 所以，其中一個很明顯的好處，就是字典能幫助孩子擴充詞彙，並學會在學習上更獨立。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one clear benefit is that dictionaries help children build
vocabulary and learn how to stand on their own feet academically.
[所以，其中一個很明顯的好處，就是字典能幫助孩子擴充詞彙，並學會在學習上更獨立。]

components.english: On top of that, dictionaries can improve children’s writing, reading, and overall language awareness.
components.chinese: 除此之外，字典也能提升孩子的寫作、閱讀，以及整體語言敏感度。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: On top of that, dictionaries can improve children’s writing, reading,
and overall language awareness.
[除此之外，字典也能提升孩子的寫作、閱讀，以及整體語言敏感度。]

components.english: To be honest, language is full of fine differences, and dictionaries help children notice them. They can see that similar words do not always mean exactly the same thing, and that one word may have several meanings depending on the context. That kind of awareness sharpens their thinking as well as their language. In other words, dictionaries do more than explain words; they teach children to pay attention to nuance.
components.chinese: 老實說，語言裡充滿細微差別，而字典正正能幫助孩子留意到這些分別。他們會開始明白，看起來相近的詞未必完全一樣，而同一個詞在不同語境裡也可能有不同意思。這種敏感度不只會磨練他們的語言能力，也會令他們的思考更細緻。換句話說，字典不只是解釋字詞；它其實也在教孩子留意語言中的細節和層次。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: To be honest, language is full of fine differences, and
dictionaries help children notice them. They can see that similar words do not
always mean exactly the same thing, and that one word may have several
meanings depending on the context. That kind of awareness sharpens their
thinking as well as their language. In other words, dictionaries do more than
explain words; they teach children to pay attention to nuance.
[老實說，語言裡充滿細微差別，而字典正正能幫助孩子留意到這些分別。他們會開始明白
，看起來相近的詞未必完全一樣，而同一個詞在不同語境裡也可能有不同意思。這種敏感度
不只會磨練他們的語言能力，也會令他們的思考更細緻。換句話說，字典不只是解釋字詞；
它其實也在教孩子留意語言中的細節和層次。]

components.english: For instance, a child may look up words like “slim,” “thin,” and “skinny” and realise that they are similar, but not identical in tone. That small discovery can help them choose words more carefully in writing and understand other people’s meaning more clearly when reading.
components.chinese: 例如，一個孩子查到 “slim”、“thin” 和 “skinny” 這些字時，可能會發現它們意思接近，但語氣和感覺並不完全相同。這種小小的發現，能幫助他在寫作時更小心地選字，也能在閱讀時更準確地理解別人的意思。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, a child may look up words like “slim,” “thin,” and
“skinny” and realise that they are similar, but not identical in tone. That small
discovery can help them choose words more carefully in writing and understand
other people’s meaning more clearly when reading.
[例如，一個孩子查到 “slim”、“thin” 和 “skinny” 這些字時，可能會發現它們意思接近，但語
氣和感覺並不完全相同。這種小小的發現，能幫助他在寫作時更小心地選字，也能在閱讀時
更準確地理解別人的意思。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say children benefit from using dictionaries because dictionaries make them more independent, more precise, and more thoughtful with language. They do not just help children find words; they help children grow into stronger learners.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說孩子使用字典之所以有好處，是因為字典能讓他們在語言上變得更獨立、更準確，也更有思考。字典不只是幫孩子找到字詞；它其實也在幫孩子成長為更扎實的學習者。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say children benefit from using dictionaries because
dictionaries make them more independent, more precise, and more thoughtful
with language. They do not just help children find words; they help children
grow into stronger learners.
[總結來說，我會說孩子使用字典之所以有好處，是因為字典能讓他們在語言上變得更獨
立、更準確，也更有思考。字典不只是幫孩子找到字詞；它其實也在幫孩子成長為更扎實的
學習者。]

question.english: In general, do you think planning is important?
question.chinese: 整體來說，你認為計劃重要嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: In general, do you think planning is important?
整體來說，你認為計劃重要嗎？

components.english: Yes, absolutely, I think planning is important in general, because it helps people use their time, money, and energy more wisely.
components.chinese: 是的，絕對重要。我認為整體來說，計劃是很重要的，因為它能幫助人更明智地運用時間、金錢，以及精力。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, absolutely, I think planning is important in general, because it helps
people use their time, money, and energy more wisely.
[是的，絕對重要。我認為整體來說，計劃是很重要的，因為它能幫助人更明智地運用時間、
金錢，以及精力。]

components.english: The main reason is that without some kind of plan, life can become too reactive. People may drift through the day, lose sight of what matters most, or end up spending all their time putting out fires. Planning does not make life perfect, of course, but it gives people a clearer direction. In that sense, it is a bit like having a map: you may still run into delays or wrong turns, but at least you are not wandering blindly.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，如果一個人完全沒有任何計劃，生活就很容易變得過度被動。人可能會渾渾噩噩地過完一天、看不清甚麼才是最重要的事情，或者把所有時間都花在不停補救各種問題上。計劃當然不能令人生變得完美，但它能讓人有更清楚的方向。從這個角度來看，計劃有點像一張地圖：途中你仍然可能會遇到延誤，或者走錯路，但至少你不是在盲目亂走。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that without some kind of plan, life can
become too reactive. People may drift through the day, lose sight of what
matters most, or end up spending all their time putting out fires. Planning does
not make life perfect, of course, but it gives people a clearer direction. In that
sense, it is a bit like having a map: you may still run into delays or wrong turns,
but at least you are not wandering blindly.
[主要原因是，如果一個人完全沒有任何計劃，生活就很容易變得過度被動。人可能會渾渾
噩噩地過完一天、看不清甚麼才是最重要的事情，或者把所有時間都花在不停補救各種問
題上。計劃當然不能令人生變得完美，但它能讓人有更清楚的方向。從這個角度來看，計劃
有點像一張地圖：途中你仍然可能會遇到延誤，或者走錯路，但至少你不是在盲目亂走。]

components.english: For example, if a student plans their week properly, they are much more likely to balance revision, homework, and rest. In the same way, if a family plans its spending carefully, it is less likely to run into unnecessary stress at the end of the month.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一位學生能夠好好安排自己的一星期，他通常就更有可能同時兼顧溫習、功課，以及休息。同樣地，如果一個家庭能夠仔細規劃開支，到月底時就較不容易出現一些本來可以避免的壓力。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if a student plans their week properly, they are much
more likely to balance revision, homework, and rest. In the same way, if a family
plans its spending carefully, it is less likely to run into unnecessary stress at the
end of the month.
[例如，如果一位學生能夠好好安排自己的一星期，他通常就更有可能同時兼顧溫習、功課，
以及休息。同樣地，如果一個家庭能夠仔細規劃開支，到月底時就較不容易出現一些本來可
以避免的壓力。]

components.english: So yes, I would say planning is important, because it turns vague intentions into clearer action and makes life more manageable.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我會說計劃是重要的，因為它能把模糊的想法轉化成更清楚的行動，也讓生活變得更容易掌握。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, I would say planning is important, because it turns vague
intentions into clearer action and makes life more manageable.
[所以，是的，我會說計劃是重要的，因為它能把模糊的想法轉化成更清楚的行動，也讓生活
變得更容易掌握。]

components.english: That said, I do not think planning should be so rigid that people cannot adjust when life changes unexpectedly.
components.chinese: 不過，我也不認為計劃應該僵硬到令人一遇到變化就完全無法調整。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think planning should be so rigid that people
cannot adjust when life changes unexpectedly.
[不過，我也不認為計劃應該僵硬到令人一遇到變化就完全無法調整。]

components.english: To be honest, life does not always follow a script. Sometimes things change suddenly, and if a person clings too tightly to every detail of a plan, they may become more stressed rather than less. A good plan should give structure, not become a straitjacket. In other words, planning works best when it leaves a little room to breathe.
components.chinese: 老實說，人生並不會永遠照著劇本走。有時事情會突然改變，而如果一個人把計劃的每個細節都抓得太緊，最後反而可能變得更焦慮，而不是更安心。一個好的計劃應該提供結構，而不是變成束縛人的枷鎖。換句話說，計劃最有效的時候，往往是它本身也留有一點可以呼吸和調整的空間。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: To be honest, life does not always follow a script. Sometimes
things change suddenly, and if a person clings too tightly to every detail of a
plan, they may become more stressed rather than less. A good plan should give
structure, not become a straitjacket. In other words, planning works best when
it leaves a little room to breathe.
[老實說，人生並不會永遠照著劇本走。有時事情會突然改變，而如果一個人把計劃的每個
細節都抓得太緊，最後反而可能變得更焦慮，而不是更安心。一個好的計劃應該提供結構，
而不是變成束縛人的枷鎖。換句話說，計劃最有效的時候，往往是它本身也留有一點可以呼
吸和調整的空間。]

components.english: For instance, someone may plan a very productive day, but then an urgent phone call, a traffic delay, or a family matter changes everything. In that case, the sensible thing is not to panic, but to reshuffle the plan and move on.
components.chinese: 例如，一個人本來可能安排了一個很高效率的一天，但突然來了一通緊急電話、遇上交通延誤，或者家裡有事，整個安排就被打亂了。在那種情況下，明智的做法不是慌張，而是重新調整計劃，然後繼續往前走。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, someone may plan a very productive day, but then an
urgent phone call, a traffic delay, or a family matter changes everything. In that
case, the sensible thing is not to panic, but to reshuffle the plan and move on.
[例如，一個人本來可能安排了一個很高效率的一天，但突然來了一通緊急電話、遇上交通
延誤，或者家裡有事，整個安排就被打亂了。在那種情況下，明智的做法不是慌張，而是重
新調整計劃，然後繼續往前走。]

components.english: Overall, I think planning is very important, but the best planning is flexible rather than rigid. A good plan should guide your life, not handcuff it.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我認為計劃非常重要，不過最好的計劃應該是有彈性的，而不是僵硬死板的。好的計劃應該引導人生，而不是把人生綁住。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I think planning is very important, but the best
planning is flexible rather than rigid. A good plan should guide your life, not
handcuff it.
[總結來說，我認為計劃非常重要，不過最好的計劃應該是有彈性的，而不是僵硬死板的。好
的計劃應該引導人生，而不是把人生綁住。]

question.english: Do you think people should make highly detailed plans or just general plans?
question.chinese: 你認為人們應該制定非常詳細的計劃，還是只制定大方向的計劃？

source_lines.text: Do you think people should make highly detailed plans or just general plans?
你認為人們應該制定非常詳細的計劃，還是只制定大方向的計劃？

components.english: Personally, I’d say general plans are usually better overall, as long as they are clear enough to give people direction.
components.chinese: 就我個人而言，我會說大方向的計劃通常整體上更好，只要它們清晰得足以給人一個方向就可以了。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Personally, I’d say general plans are usually better overall, as long as they
are clear enough to give people direction.
[就我個人而言，我會說大方向的計劃通常整體上更好，只要它們清晰得足以給人一個方向
就可以了。]

components.english: The main reason is that life rarely sticks to the script. Highly detailed plans may look neat on paper, but real life has a habit of throwing curveballs. If every step is planned too tightly, people can become stressed or frustrated the moment something changes. By contrast, a general plan is more like a compass than a cage: it points you in the right direction without boxing you in.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，人生很少真的會完全照著劇本走。非常詳細的計劃在紙上看起來也許很整齊，但現實生活往往喜歡突然拋出一些變數。如果每一步都計劃得太死，一旦事情有變，人就很容易感到壓力或挫敗。相比之下，大方向的計劃更像指南針，而不是牢籠：它會指引你往正確方向走，但不會把你困住。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that life rarely sticks to the script. Highly
detailed plans may look neat on paper, but real life has a habit of throwing
curveballs. If every step is planned too tightly, people can become stressed or
frustrated the moment something changes. By contrast, a general plan is more
like a compass than a cage: it points you in the right direction without boxing
you in.
[主要原因是，人生很少真的會完全照著劇本走。非常詳細的計劃在紙上看起來也許很整齊
，但現實生活往往喜歡突然拋出一些變數。如果每一步都計劃得太死，一旦事情有變，人就
很容易感到壓力或挫敗。相比之下，大方向的計劃更像指南針，而不是牢籠：它會指引你往
正確方向走，但不會把你困住。]

components.english: For example, if a student is preparing for an exam, it may be more helpful to make a broad weekly plan, such as revising certain subjects on certain days, rather than planning every hour down to the last minute. That way, if something unexpected comes up, they can adapt without feeling that the whole plan has fallen apart.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一位學生正在準備考試，那麼制定一個較概括的每週計劃，例如在哪幾天溫習哪些科目，往往會比把每一個小時都安排得死死的更有幫助。這樣一來，如果突然有事情發生，他也可以靈活調整，而不會覺得整個計劃都徹底崩掉了。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if a student is preparing for an exam, it may be more
helpful to make a broad weekly plan, such as revising certain subjects on certain
days, rather than planning every hour down to the last minute. That way, if
something unexpected comes up, they can adapt without feeling that the whole
plan has fallen apart.
[例如，如果一位學生正在準備考試，那麼制定一個較概括的每週計劃，例如在哪幾天溫習
哪些科目，往往會比把每一個小時都安排得死死的更有幫助。這樣一來，如果突然有事情發
生，他也可以靈活調整，而不會覺得整個計劃都徹底崩掉了。]

components.english: So, in most everyday situations, I think general plans work better because they provide structure without squeezing out flexibility.
components.chinese: 所以，在大多數日常情況下，我認為大方向的計劃會更好，因為它既能提供結構，又不會把彈性完全擠走。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, in most everyday situations, I think general plans work better
because they provide structure without squeezing out flexibility.
[所以，在大多數日常情況下，我認為大方向的計劃會更好，因為它既能提供結構，又不會
把彈性完全擠走。]

components.english: That said, highly detailed plans do have their place, especially when the task is important, complex, or time-sensitive.
components.chinese: 不過，非常詳細的計劃也確實有它的用處，尤其是當事情本身很重要、很複雜，或者時間性很強的時候。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, highly detailed plans do have their place, especially when
the task is important, complex, or time-sensitive.
[不過，非常詳細的計劃也確實有它的用處，尤其是當事情本身很重要、很複雜，或者時間
性很強的時候。]

components.english: In those cases, a vague plan may simply not be enough. If people are organising a wedding, running a business project, or planning a trip with a tight budget, details matter. They need to know who is doing what, when things need to happen, and what the backup plan is. In other words, general plans are good for movement, but detailed plans are often necessary for precision.
components.chinese: 在這些情況下，太模糊的計劃可能根本不夠。如果人們正在籌備婚禮、處理商業項目，或者安排一趟預算很緊的旅行，那麼細節就真的很重要了。他們需要知道誰負責甚麼、事情甚麼時候要完成，以及後備方案是甚麼。換句話說，大方向的計劃有利於推進事情，但詳細計劃往往是精準執行所必須的。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: In those cases, a vague plan may simply not be enough. If
people are organising a wedding, running a business project, or planning a trip
with a tight budget, details matter. They need to know who is doing what,
when things need to happen, and what the backup plan is. In other words,
general plans are good for movement, but detailed plans are often necessary for
precision.
[在這些情況下，太模糊的計劃可能根本不夠。如果人們正在籌備婚禮、處理商業項目，或
者安排一趟預算很緊的旅行，那麼細節就真的很重要了。他們需要知道誰負責甚麼、事情甚
麼時候要完成，以及後備方案是甚麼。換句話說，大方向的計劃有利於推進事情，但詳細計
劃往往是精準執行所必須的。]

components.english: For instance, if a team is preparing for a major event, it cannot just say, “We’ll sort it out somehow.” It needs a proper schedule, clear responsibilities, and deadlines. Otherwise, things can slip through the cracks and the whole plan may unravel at the last minute.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個團隊正在籌備一場大型活動，它不能只是說「到時再算吧」。它需要一個完整的時間表、清楚的分工，以及明確的截止日期。否則，一些事情很容易會漏掉，而整個安排也可能在最後一刻散掉。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, if a team is preparing for a major event, it cannot
just say, “We’ll sort it out somehow.” It needs a proper schedule, clear
responsibilities, and deadlines. Otherwise, things can slip through the cracks
and the whole plan may unravel at the last minute.
[例如，如果一個團隊正在籌備一場大型活動，它不能只是說「到時再算吧」。它需要一個完
整的時間表、清楚的分工，以及明確的截止日期。否則，一些事情很容易會漏掉，而整個安
排也可能在最後一刻散掉。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say general plans are better for most people most of the time, because they leave room to breathe and adapt. However, when the stakes are high, detailed plans can be the difference between keeping things on track and letting them drift off course.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說對大多數人而言，在大多數情況下，大方向的計劃會更好，因為它讓人有空間去調整，也有餘地去呼吸。不過，當事情的代價很高時，詳細計劃往往就可能成為事情能否保持正軌、還是慢慢偏離方向的關鍵。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say general plans are better for most people most of
the time, because they leave room to breathe and adapt. However, when the
stakes are high, detailed plans can be the difference between keeping things on
track and letting them drift off course.
[總結來說，我會說對大多數人而言，在大多數情況下，大方向的計劃會更好，因為它讓人
有空間去調整，也有餘地去呼吸。不過，當事情的代價很高時，詳細計劃往往就可能成為事
情能否保持正軌、還是慢慢偏離方向的關鍵。]

question.english: What types of people like to make plans?
question.chinese: 甚麼類型的人喜歡做計劃？

source_lines.text: Topic: What types of people like to make plans?
甚麼類型的人喜歡做計劃？

components.english: I think people who are organised, responsible, and goal-oriented are usually the ones who like to make plans.
components.chinese: 我認為，那些有條理、有責任感，而且很有目標感的人，通常都比較喜歡做計劃。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think people who are organised, responsible, and goal-oriented are
usually the ones who like to make plans.
[我認為，那些有條理、有責任感，而且很有目標感的人，通常都比較喜歡做計劃。]

components.english: The main reason is that planning gives them a sense of direction and control. They do not like leaving everything to chance, especially if they have a lot on their plate. For that kind of person, a plan is not just a schedule; it is a map. It helps them see what needs to be done, when it should be done, and how to avoid things falling through the cracks.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，計劃能給他們一種方向感和掌控感。他們不喜歡把一切都交給運氣，尤其是當自己手頭上本來就有很多事情要處理的時候。對這類人來說，計劃不只是一張時間表，而更像是一張地圖。它能幫助他們看清楚甚麼事情要做、甚麼時候做，以及怎樣避免事情從手縫裡溜走。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that planning gives them a sense of direction
and control. They do not like leaving everything to chance, especially if they
have a lot on their plate. For that kind of person, a plan is not just a schedule; it
is a map. It helps them see what needs to be done, when it should be done, and
how to avoid things falling through the cracks.
[主要原因是，計劃能給他們一種方向感和掌控感。他們不喜歡把一切都交給運氣，尤其是
當自己手頭上本來就有很多事情要處理的時候。對這類人來說，計劃不只是一張時間表，而
更像是一張地圖。它能幫助他們看清楚甚麼事情要做、甚麼時候做，以及怎樣避免事情從手
縫裡溜走。]

components.english: For example, students who want good grades, professionals who handle many deadlines, or parents who need to juggle work and family life often like making plans. Without some kind of structure, their day could easily turn into a mess.
components.chinese: 例如，那些想考好成績的學生、要應付很多期限的上班族，或者要同時兼顧工作和家庭的父母，很多時都會喜歡做計劃。沒有某種程度的安排，他們的一天很容易就會亂成一團。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, students who want good grades, professionals who
handle many deadlines, or parents who need to juggle work and family life often
like making plans. Without some kind of structure, their day could easily turn
into a mess.
[例如，那些想考好成績的學生、要應付很多期限的上班族，或者要同時兼顧工作和家庭的
父母，很多時都會喜歡做計劃。沒有某種程度的安排，他們的一天很容易就會亂成一團。]

components.english: So, one major type of person who likes to make plans is someone who values order, efficiency, and clear progress.
components.chinese: 所以，其中一種很喜歡做計劃的人，通常就是那些重視秩序、效率，以及清晰進展的人。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one major type of person who likes to make plans is someone
who values order, efficiency, and clear progress.
[所以，其中一種很喜歡做計劃的人，通常就是那些重視秩序、效率，以及清晰進展的人。]

components.english: That said, it is not only highly organised people who like planning; sometimes anxious or cautious people do too.
components.chinese: 不過，喜歡做計劃的人不一定只有那些特別有條理的人；有時候，較焦慮或較謹慎的人也一樣會很喜歡。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, it is not only highly organised people who like planning;
sometimes anxious or cautious people do too.
[不過，喜歡做計劃的人不一定只有那些特別有條理的人；有時候，較焦慮或較謹慎的人也
一樣會很喜歡。]

components.english: Some people make plans because they genuinely enjoy structure, while others do it because uncertainty makes them uncomfortable. A plan can calm their nerves and make the future feel less foggy. In other words, planning can be both a tool for success and a form of emotional comfort. That is why careful travellers, worriers, or people who dislike surprises often plan everything down to the last detail.
components.chinese: 有些人做計劃，是因為他們真的享受那種有結構的感覺；而另一些人則是因為不確定性會令他們不安。一個計劃可以安撫他們的情緒，也能令未來看起來不那麼模糊。換句話說，計劃既可以是一種通往成功的工具，也可以是一種情緒上的安穩感。正因如此，那些很小心的旅客、容易擔心的人，或者很討厭驚喜的人，很多時都會把事情安排得細緻到幾乎一絲不漏。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: Some people make plans because they genuinely enjoy
structure, while others do it because uncertainty makes them uncomfortable. A
plan can calm their nerves and make the future feel less foggy. In other words,
planning can be both a tool for success and a form of emotional comfort. That is
why careful travellers, worriers, or people who dislike surprises often plan
everything down to the last detail.
[有些人做計劃，是因為他們真的享受那種有結構的感覺；而另一些人則是因為不確定性會
令他們不安。一個計劃可以安撫他們的情緒，也能令未來看起來不那麼模糊。換句話說，計
劃既可以是一種通往成功的工具，也可以是一種情緒上的安穩感。正因如此，那些很小心的
旅客、容易擔心的人，或者很討厭驚喜的人，很多時都會把事情安排得細緻到幾乎一絲不

components.english: For instance, before a trip, one person may simply pack a bag and go, whereas another may prepare a full itinerary, check transport options, book everything in advance, and even make a backup plan. The second type is often someone who feels better when life has a blueprint.
components.chinese: 例如，在旅行之前，有些人可能只是隨便收拾一下就出發；但另一些人則會先做好完整行程、查清交通安排、提早訂好所有東西，甚至連後備方案都準備好。第二種人很多時就是那種只要人生有藍圖，心裡就會更踏實的人。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, before a trip, one person may simply pack a bag and
go, whereas another may prepare a full itinerary, check transport options, book
everything in advance, and even make a backup plan. The second type is often
someone who feels better when life has a blueprint.
[例如，在旅行之前，有些人可能只是隨便收拾一下就出發；但另一些人則會先做好完整行
程、查清交通安排、提早訂好所有東西，甚至連後備方案都準備好。第二種人很多時就是那
種只要人生有藍圖，心裡就會更踏實的人。]

components.english: Overall, I would say people who like to make plans are usually either naturally organised or emotionally comforted by structure. Of course, that does not mean people who dislike planning are careless; some simply prefer to play things by ear. But in general, planners tend to be the people who feel more secure when life has shape rather than blur.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，喜歡做計劃的人通常不是天生較有條理，就是會從結構感之中得到情緒上的安穩。當然，這並不代表不喜歡做計劃的人就一定粗心；有些人只是更喜歡見步行步而已。不過整體來說，喜歡規劃的人往往就是那些在人生有輪廓而不是一片模糊時，會感到更安心的人。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, I would say people who like to make plans are usually
either naturally organised or emotionally comforted by structure. Of course,
that does not mean people who dislike planning are careless; some simply prefer
to play things by ear. But in general, planners tend to be the people who feel
more secure when life has shape rather than blur.
[總結來說，我會說，喜歡做計劃的人通常不是天生較有條理，就是會從結構感之中得到情
緒上的安穩。當然，這並不代表不喜歡做計劃的人就一定粗心；有些人只是更喜歡見步行步
而已。不過整體來說，喜歡規劃的人往往就是那些在人生有輪廓而不是一片模糊時，會感到
更安心的人。]

question.english: Why do you think some people dislike making plans?
question.chinese: 你認為為甚麼有些人不喜歡做計劃？

source_lines.text: Topic: Why do you think some people dislike making plans?
你認為為甚麼有些人不喜歡做計劃？

components.english: I think many people dislike making plans because plans can make life feel too rigid, and some people simply prefer to play things by ear.
components.chinese: 我認為，很多人不喜歡做計劃，是因為計劃有時會令生活顯得太死板，而有些人本來就比較喜歡隨機應變、順其自然。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think many people dislike making plans because plans can make life
feel too rigid, and some people simply prefer to play things by ear.
[我認為，很多人不喜歡做計劃，是因為計劃有時會令生活顯得太死板，而有些人本來就比
較喜歡隨機應變、順其自然。]

components.english: For certain personalities, planning feels less like security and more like a cage. When everything is mapped out in advance, spontaneity can disappear, and even something enjoyable can start to feel over-managed. Some people like having the freedom to change their mind, follow their mood, or take opportunities as they come. In that sense, they do not see planning as helpful structure; they see it as something that boxes them in.
components.chinese: 對某些性格的人來說，計劃帶來的感覺不是安全感，反而更像一個框架，甚至像一個籠子。當所有事情都預先安排好時，那種即興和自然感就很容易消失，就連本來開心的事情也可能開始變得像被管理過頭。有些人喜歡保留改變主意的自由、跟著當下心情走，或者在機會出現時再即場把握。從這個角度來看，他們不是覺得計劃提供了有用的秩序，而是覺得它把自己困住了。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: For certain personalities, planning feels less like security and
more like a cage. When everything is mapped out in advance, spontaneity can
disappear, and even something enjoyable can start to feel over-managed. Some
people like having the freedom to change their mind, follow their mood, or take
opportunities as they come. In that sense, they do not see planning as helpful
structure; they see it as something that boxes them in.
[對某些性格的人來說，計劃帶來的感覺不是安全感，反而更像一個框架，甚至像一個籠
子。當所有事情都預先安排好時，那種即興和自然感就很容易消失，就連本來開心的事情也
可能開始變得像被管理過頭。有些人喜歡保留改變主意的自由、跟著當下心情走，或者在機
會出現時再即場把握。從這個角度來看，他們不是覺得計劃提供了有用的秩序，而是覺得它
把自己困住了。]

components.english: For example, if a group of friends is planning a day out, one person may want a full schedule from morning to night, while another may say, “Let’s just see how we feel.” That second person is not necessarily irresponsible; they may simply enjoy a looser and more natural rhythm.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一群朋友要去玩一天，其中一個人可能想把早上到晚上的行程都安排好，但另一個人則可能會說：「到時再看看心情吧。」後者不一定是不負責任，他可能只是單純比較喜歡一種較鬆動、較自然的節奏。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if a group of friends is planning a day out, one person
may want a full schedule from morning to night, while another may say, “Let’s
just see how we feel.” That second person is not necessarily irresponsible; they
may simply enjoy a looser and more natural rhythm.
[例如，如果一群朋友要去玩一天，其中一個人可能想把早上到晚上的行程都安排好，但另
一個人則可能會說：「到時再看看心情吧。」後者不一定是不負責任，他可能只是單純比較喜
歡一種較鬆動、較自然的節奏。]

components.english: So, one clear reason some people dislike making plans is that planning can feel like control rather than comfort.
components.chinese: 所以，有些人不喜歡做計劃，其中一個很明顯的原因，就是計劃對他們來說感覺像被控制，而不是被照顧。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one clear reason some people dislike making plans is that
planning can feel like control rather than comfort.
[所以，有些人不喜歡做計劃，其中一個很明顯的原因，就是計劃對他們來說感覺像被控制
，而不是被照顧。]

components.english: Another reason is that making plans can create pressure, especially for people whose lives are already busy or unpredictable.
components.chinese: 另一個原因是，做計劃本身可能會帶來壓力，特別是對那些生活已經很忙，或者本來就充滿不確定性的人來說。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: Another reason is that making plans can create pressure, especially for
people whose lives are already busy or unpredictable.
[另一個原因是，做計劃本身可能會帶來壓力，特別是對那些生活已經很忙，或者本來就充
滿不確定性的人來說。]

components.english: Once a plan is written down, it can start to feel like a promise. If something changes, people may feel guilty, frustrated, or as though they have failed in some small way. That is why some people would rather stay flexible than pin everything down too early. To be honest, if someone’s schedule is constantly shifting because of work, family, or other responsibilities, planning can feel a bit like building on sand.
components.chinese: 一旦計劃寫了下來，它有時就會開始像一種承諾。如果之後情況有變，人就可能會感到內疚、煩躁，或者好像自己某程度上失敗了一樣。所以，有些人寧願保持彈性，也不想太早把所有事情釘死。老實說，如果一個人的時間表本來就因為工作、家庭，或者其他責任而不停變動，那麼做計劃有時真的會像在沙上建房子一樣。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: Once a plan is written down, it can start to feel like a promise. If
something changes, people may feel guilty, frustrated, or as though they have
failed in some small way. That is why some people would rather stay flexible
than pin everything down too early. To be honest, if someone’s schedule is
constantly shifting because of work, family, or other responsibilities, planning
can feel a bit like building on sand.
[一旦計劃寫了下來，它有時就會開始像一種承諾。如果之後情況有變，人就可能會感到內
疚、煩躁，或者好像自己某程度上失敗了一樣。所以，有些人寧願保持彈性，也不想太早把
所有事情釘死。老實說，如果一個人的時間表本來就因為工作、家庭，或者其他責任而不停
變動，那麼做計劃有時真的會像在沙上建房子一樣。]

components.english: For instance, a parent with young children or someone with a very demanding job may avoid making detailed weekend plans, because they already know things may change at the last minute. In that case, not planning too much is not laziness; it is self-protection.
components.chinese: 例如，一位要照顧年幼孩子的家長，或者一位工作非常繁忙的人，可能會避免把週末安排得太仔細，因為他們本來就知道事情很可能在最後一刻改變。在這種情況下，不把事情計劃得太滿，不是因為懶，而是一種保護自己心情的方式。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, a parent with young children or someone with a very
demanding job may avoid making detailed weekend plans, because they already
know things may change at the last minute. In that case, not planning too much
is not laziness; it is self-protection.
[例如，一位要照顧年幼孩子的家長，或者一位工作非常繁忙的人，可能會避免把週末安排
得太仔細，因為他們本來就知道事情很可能在最後一刻改變。在這種情況下，不把事情計劃
得太滿，不是因為懶，而是一種保護自己心情的方式。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say some people dislike making plans because they either value spontaneity more or feel that plans bring too much pressure. For them, life feels easier when it is guided, not tightly scripted.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，有些人不喜歡做計劃，是因為他們不是更重視即興感，就是覺得計劃會帶來太大壓力。對他們來說，人生比較適合被引導，而不是被寫成一份太死的劇本。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say some people dislike making plans because they
either value spontaneity more or feel that plans bring too much pressure. For
them, life feels easier when it is guided, not tightly scripted.
[總結來說，我會說，有些人不喜歡做計劃，是因為他們不是更重視即興感，就是覺得計劃
會帶來太大壓力。對他們來說，人生比較適合被引導，而不是被寫成一份太死的劇本。]

question.english: Do you think it’s important for a person to have a career plan?
question.chinese: 你認為一個人擁有職業規劃重要嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Do you think it’s important for a person to have a career plan?
[你認為一個人擁有職業規劃重要嗎？]

components.english: Yes, I do think it is important for a person to have a career plan, because without some sense of direction, it is very easy to drift rather than move forward with purpose.
components.chinese: 是的，我的確認為一個人有職業規劃是重要的，因為如果沒有某種方向感，人就很容易只是隨波逐流，而不是有目標地向前走。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, I do think it is important for a person to have a career plan, because
without some sense of direction, it is very easy to drift rather than move
forward with purpose.
[是的，我的確認為一個人有職業規劃是重要的，因為如果沒有某種方向感，人就很容易只
是隨波逐流，而不是有目標地向前走。]

components.english: A career plan gives people something to aim at. It helps them decide what skills to build, what opportunities to take seriously, and what kind of work may suit them in the long run. In that sense, a career plan is a bit like a compass: it does not map out every single step, but it stops people from going round in circles. Without one, some people may spend years being busy without really getting anywhere.
components.chinese: 職業規劃能給人一個努力的方向。它幫助人判斷自己應該培養甚麼技能、應該認真把握哪些機會，以及長遠來說，甚麼樣的工作較適合自己。從這個角度來說，職業規劃有點像指南針：它未必替你畫好每一步，但它能避免你一直在原地兜圈。沒有它的話，有些人可能會忙很多年，卻始終沒有真正走到哪裡。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: A career plan gives people something to aim at. It helps them
decide what skills to build, what opportunities to take seriously, and what kind
of work may suit them in the long run. In that sense, a career plan is a bit like a
compass: it does not map out every single step, but it stops people from going
round in circles. Without one, some people may spend years being busy without
really getting anywhere.
[職業規劃能給人一個努力的方向。它幫助人判斷自己應該培養甚麼技能、應該認真把握哪
些機會，以及長遠來說，甚麼樣的工作較適合自己。從這個角度來說，職業規劃有點像指南
針：它未必替你畫好每一步，但它能避免你一直在原地兜圈。沒有它的話，有些人可能會忙
很多年，卻始終沒有真正走到哪裡。]

components.english: For example, if a university student hopes to work in marketing, having a career plan may push them to choose relevant courses, gain internship experience, and improve their communication skills early on. That kind of planning can give them a clearer path instead of leaving everything to chance.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個大學生希望將來從事市場推廣工作，那麼有職業規劃就可能會推動他提早選修相關課程、爭取實習經驗，以及提升自己的溝通能力。這種規劃能讓他有一條更清晰的路，而不是把一切都交給運氣。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if a university student hopes to work in marketing,
having a career plan may push them to choose relevant courses, gain internship
experience, and improve their communication skills early on. That kind of
planning can give them a clearer path instead of leaving everything to chance.
[例如，如果一個大學生希望將來從事市場推廣工作，那麼有職業規劃就可能會推動他提早
選修相關課程、爭取實習經驗，以及提升自己的溝通能力。這種規劃能讓他有一條更清晰的
路，而不是把一切都交給運氣。]

components.english: So yes, I would say having a career plan is important, because it gives people focus, direction, and a stronger sense of purpose.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我會說擁有職業規劃是重要的，因為它能帶給人專注、方向感，以及更強的目標意識。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, I would say having a career plan is important, because it
gives people focus, direction, and a stronger sense of purpose.
[所以，是的，我會說擁有職業規劃是重要的，因為它能帶給人專注、方向感，以及更強的目
標意識。]

components.english: That said, I do not think a career plan should be too rigid, because life does not always move in a straight line.
components.chinese: 不過，我也不認為職業規劃應該過於僵化，因為人生未必總是沿著一條直線前進。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think a career plan should be too rigid, because life
does not always move in a straight line.
[不過，我也不認為職業規劃應該過於僵化，因為人生未必總是沿著一條直線前進。]

components.english: Sometimes people discover new interests, unexpected strengths, or opportunities they never saw coming. If someone clings too tightly to one fixed plan, they may miss a better road simply because it was not part of the original script. So, to me, the best career plan is not a cage; it is a framework. It should guide people, not trap them. A plan matters, but so does the ability to adapt when the ground shifts under your feet.
components.chinese: 有時候，人會發現新的興趣、意想不到的長處，或者一些原本根本沒有預料到的機會。如果一個人把自己死死綁在某個固定計劃上，他就可能會錯過一條更好的路，只因為那不在原來的劇本裡。所以對我來說，最好的職業規劃不是牢籠，而是一個框架。它應該引導人，而不是困住人。規劃固然重要，但當腳下的地面開始移動時，懂得調整同樣重要。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: Sometimes people discover new interests, unexpected
strengths, or opportunities they never saw coming. If someone clings too tightly
to one fixed plan, they may miss a better road simply because it was not part of
the original script. So, to me, the best career plan is not a cage; it is a framework.
It should guide people, not trap them. A plan matters, but so does the ability to
adapt when the ground shifts under your feet.
[有時候，人會發現新的興趣、意想不到的長處，或者一些原本根本沒有預料到的機會。如
果一個人把自己死死綁在某個固定計劃上，他就可能會錯過一條更好的路，只因為那不在
原來的劇本裡。所以對我來說，最好的職業規劃不是牢籠，而是一個框架。它應該引導人，
而不是困住人。規劃固然重要，但當腳下的地面開始移動時，懂得調整同樣重要。]

components.english: For instance, someone may begin by planning to become a teacher, but later realise they are better suited to educational technology, counselling, or curriculum design. In that case, changing direction would not mean the plan failed; it would mean the person grew into a better one.
components.chinese: 例如，一個人起初可能打算成為老師，但後來卻發現自己其實更適合教育科技、輔導，或者課程設計。在這種情況下，改變方向並不代表原來的規劃失敗了；它反而代表這個人慢慢長成了更適合自己的版本。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, someone may begin by planning to become a teacher,
but later realise they are better suited to educational technology, counselling, or
curriculum design. In that case, changing direction would not mean the plan
failed; it would mean the person grew into a better one.
[例如，一個人起初可能打算成為老師，但後來卻發現自己其實更適合教育科技、輔導，或
者課程設計。在這種情況下，改變方向並不代表原來的規劃失敗了；它反而代表這個人慢慢
長成了更適合自己的版本。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say it is important to have a career plan, but it should be flexible rather than set in stone. The best plan is one that gives you direction, while still leaving the door open for growth, change, and better possibilities.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說有職業規劃是重要的，但那份規劃應該有彈性，而不是一成不變。最好的規劃，是那種既能給你方向，同時又為成長、改變，以及更好的可能性留下一道門的規劃。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, I’d say it is important to have a career plan, but it should
be flexible rather than set in stone. The best plan is one that gives you direction,
while still leaving the door open for growth, change, and better possibilities.
[總結來說，我會說有職業規劃是重要的，但那份規劃應該有彈性，而不是一成不變。最好
的規劃，是那種既能給你方向，同時又為成長、改變，以及更好的可能性留下一道門的規

question.english: How do most people plan their futures in their education and careers?
question.chinese: 人們通常怎樣規劃自己在教育和事業上的未來？

source_lines.text: Topic: How do most people plan their futures in their education and careers?
人們通常怎樣規劃自己在教育和事業上的未來？

components.english: I think most people plan their futures by looking at three things: what they are good at, what they enjoy, and what kind of life they want to build.
components.chinese: 我認為，大多數人規劃自己未來時，通常會先看三樣東西：自己擅長甚麼、喜歡甚麼，以及想建立一種怎樣的生活。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think most people plan their futures by looking at three things: what
they are good at, what they enjoy, and what kind of life they want to build.
[我認為，大多數人規劃自己未來時，通常會先看三樣東西：自己擅長甚麼、喜歡甚麼，以及
想建立一種怎樣的生活。]

components.english: In most cases, people do not just wake up one day and magically know the answer. They usually think about their strengths, school results, interests, and job prospects, and then try to connect the dots. Some may aim for a stable career, while others may care more about passion or personal growth. So planning often begins with a rough picture rather than a perfect map.
components.chinese: 很多時候，人並不是某一天一醒來，就突然神奇地知道答案。通常他們會先想想自己的強項、學業成績、興趣，以及工作前景，然後再慢慢把那些點連起來。有些人會比較重視穩定的職業，而另一些人則可能更在意熱情或者個人成長。所以，規劃未來很多時候其實是從一個大概的輪廓開始，而不是一張完全清楚的地圖。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: In most cases, people do not just wake up one day and magically
know the answer. They usually think about their strengths, school results,
interests, and job prospects, and then try to connect the dots. Some may aim for
a stable career, while others may care more about passion or personal growth. So
planning often begins with a rough picture rather than a perfect map.
[很多時候，人並不是某一天一醒來，就突然神奇地知道答案。通常他們會先想想自己的強
項、學業成績、興趣，以及工作前景，然後再慢慢把那些點連起來。有些人會比較重視穩定
的職業，而另一些人則可能更在意熱情或者個人成長。所以，規劃未來很多時候其實是從一
個大概的輪廓開始，而不是一張完全清楚的地圖。]

components.english: For example, a student who does well in science and also likes helping people may start thinking about medicine, nursing, or pharmacy. They might talk to teachers, ask their parents for advice, look into university courses, and choose subjects that keep those doors open.
components.chinese: 例如，一個在科學方面表現不錯，而且又喜歡幫助別人的學生，可能就會開始考慮醫學、護理，或者藥劑這些方向。他可能會跟老師談、聽聽父母的意見、了解大學課程，然後選擇一些能為自己保留這些機會的科目。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, a student who does well in science and also likes
helping people may start thinking about medicine, nursing, or pharmacy. They
might talk to teachers, ask their parents for advice, look into university courses,
and choose subjects that keep those doors open.
[例如，一個在科學方面表現不錯，而且又喜歡幫助別人的學生，可能就會開始考慮醫學、
護理，或者藥劑這些方向。他可能會跟老師談、聽聽父母的意見、了解大學課程，然後選擇
一些能為自己保留這些機會的科目。]

components.english: So, generally speaking, most people plan their education and careers by matching their interests and abilities with realistic opportunities.
components.chinese: 所以，一般來說，大多數人都是透過把自己的興趣和能力，和現實中的機會互相配對，來規劃教育和事業的。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, generally speaking, most people plan their education and
careers by matching their interests and abilities with realistic opportunities.
[所以，一般來說，大多數人都是透過把自己的興趣和能力，和現實中的機會互相配對，來
規劃教育和事業的。]

components.english: That said, I do not think most people plan everything in a rigid way, because real life has a habit of changing the script.
components.chinese: 不過，我也不覺得大多數人會用一種很僵硬的方式把一切都規劃好，因為現實生活往往很喜歡臨時改劇本。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think most people plan everything in a rigid way,
because real life has a habit of changing the script.
[不過，我也不覺得大多數人會用一種很僵硬的方式把一切都規劃好，因為現實生活往往很
喜歡臨時改劇本。]

components.english: Very often, people start with one idea and then adjust it as they grow older, gain experience, or see new opportunities. A person may discover that what looked right on paper does not feel right in real life. In that sense, planning a future is less like laying down railway tracks and more like steering a boat: you still need a direction, but you also have to respond to the water, the wind, and whatever comes your way.
components.chinese: 很多時候，人們一開始有一個想法，但之後會隨著年紀增長、經驗累積，或者看到新的機會，而慢慢調整方向。有些事情在紙上看起來好像很適合自己，但真正走進去之後，感覺卻未必對。從這個角度來說，規劃未來不像是在鋪一條完全固定的鐵路，反而更像是在掌舵開船：你當然需要方向，但你同時也得回應水流、風向，以及途中出現的變化。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: Very often, people start with one idea and then adjust it as they
grow older, gain experience, or see new opportunities. A person may discover
that what looked right on paper does not feel right in real life. In that sense,
planning a future is less like laying down railway tracks and more like steering a
boat: you still need a direction, but you also have to respond to the water, the
wind, and whatever comes your way.
[很多時候，人們一開始有一個想法，但之後會隨著年紀增長、經驗累積，或者看到新的機
會，而慢慢調整方向。有些事情在紙上看起來好像很適合自己，但真正走進去之後，感覺卻
未必對。從這個角度來說，規劃未來不像是在鋪一條完全固定的鐵路，反而更像是在掌舵開
船：你當然需要方向，但你同時也得回應水流、風向，以及途中出現的變化。]
